findings.friendlyName: Enable system-wide Exploit Protection mitigations (DEP, SEHOP, ASLR, CFG)
findings.description: Windows 11 includes Exploit Protection, the successor to EMET, which applies process-hardening mitigations (DEP, SEHOP, mandatory ASLR/bottom-up ASLR, CFG, validate exception chains, heap integrity) at boot. When the system-level mitigations are left at defaults, certain legacy mitigations (notably SEHOP and Bottom-Up ASLR) are not enforced, which makes a class of memory-corruption exploits significantly easier. Microsoft publishes a recommended baseline XML and PowerShell cmdlets to set these defaults.
findings.compatNote: Forcing mandatory ASLR or system-wide CFG can break a small number of older third-party applications (notably some games, drivers and old installers). Apply the Microsoft baseline XML rather than maxing out every option, and exempt specific apps via Set-ProcessMitigation -Name <exe> -Disable <flag> if needed.

command.description: Enables Microsoft-recommended system-wide Exploit Protection mitigations. Equivalent to importing Microsoft's baseline XML.
Snapshot current state for rollback before applying.

implementation.notes: Some flags (e.g. MicrosoftSignedOnly, BlockDynamicCode) are intentionally noisy on consumer Windows; consider enabling only DEP, SEHOP, ForceRelocateImages, BottomUp, HighEntropy, TerminateOnError, StrictHandle, CFG for a Win11 Pro home/SMB profile. Reboot recommended. Rollback: Set-ProcessMitigation -System -Disable <flag> or import the baseline snapshot.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - Customize exploit protection
Microsoft Learn - Set-ProcessMitigation cmdlet
Microsoft Learn - Enable exploit protection
Microsoft Learn - Override Process Mitigation Options (SEHOP)

findings.friendlyName: Unregister abusable URL protocol handlers (ms-msdt / search-ms / ms-search) - Follina mitigation
findings.description: CVE-2022-30190 ("Follina") and the related Windows Search protocol abuse let a Microsoft Office document trigger an external URL handler (ms-msdt: or search-ms:) that runs PowerShell or opens an attacker-controlled remote search window with arbitrary executables. Microsoft's official workaround is to back up and delete the offending HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT keys so Office and Explorer no longer hand off these URIs. Even though the June 2022 cumulative updates patched the immediate RCE in MSDT, removing the handlers eliminates the attack surface for variant techniques that still rely on the registered URI scheme.
findings.compatNote: Removing HKCR\ms-msdt disables the MSDT troubleshooter and any Microsoft tool that depends on it (DaRT, certain Get Help links, the in-box Network/Audio/Printer troubleshooters). Removing HKCR\search-ms breaks Explorer 'saved searches' (.search-ms files) and any third-party shortcut that uses the search-ms: scheme. Exporting both keys before deletion lets you restore them with reg import on demand.

command.description: Back up the ms-msdt URL handler before removing it.
Back up the search-ms URL handler before removing it.
Microsoft's official Follina workaround: unregisters the MSDT URL protocol handler.
Unregisters the Windows Search URL protocol handler abused by the related search-ms zero-day.

implementation.notes: Restore with: reg import C:\ProgramData\Win11Hardening\ms-msdt.backup.reg (and similarly for search-ms). No reboot required; effect is immediate for new processes. If Bitdefender flagged the broader 'ms-search' URI, verify HKCR\ms-search separately - on most Win11 Pro builds only ms-msdt and search-ms are registered.

references.label: Microsoft MSRC - CVE-2022-30190 guidance
Microsoft Q&A - Official ms-msdt workaround steps
Bitdefender advisory - Follina technical analysis
Bitdefender HotForSecurity - Windows Search zero-day (search-ms)
MSRC blog - turning off vulnerable protocol handlers

findings.friendlyName: Detect Hafnium / Tarrask hidden scheduled tasks (manual review)
findings.description: Tarrask is malware attributed to the Chinese Hafnium group that achieves persistence by creating a normal Windows scheduled task and then deleting the SD (Security Descriptor) value under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Schedule\TaskCache\Tree\<TaskName>. Because Task Scheduler MMC and schtasks.exe filter out tasks they cannot read, the task vanishes from every standard listing while still firing on its trigger. There is no safe automatic remediation: deleting random hidden tasks can break legitimate post-install components. The correct response is to enumerate suspicious entries and review each one before removing.
findings.compatNote: Read-only enumeration only. No system change. Any deletion must be done manually by a human after triage.

command.command: $base = 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Schedule\TaskCache\Tree'; Get-ChildItem -Path $base -Recurse -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | Where-Object { $_.PSChildName -ne $null -and -not (Get-ItemProperty -Path $_.PSPath -Name 'SD' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue) -and (Get-ItemProperty -Path $_.PSPath -Name 'Id' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue) } | Select-Object @{n='TaskKey';e={$_.PSPath -replace '.*Tree\\',''}}, @{n='Id';e={(Get-ItemProperty -Path $_.PSPath -Name Id).Id}} | Format-Table -AutoSize
command.description: Enumerates registered scheduled tasks (have an 'Id' value) that are missing the 'SD' value - the canonical Tarrask hiding technique.
Cross-reference Task Scheduler creation events (4698) and updates (4702) with what the registry currently shows; entries in the registry that have no matching 4698 are highly suspicious.

implementation.notes: DO NOT auto-delete. Human triage required. For each hit: (1) inspect the task's Actions registry value (binary blob, decode with schtasks /tn <name> /xml after temporarily restoring SD), (2) check the parent path - tasks under \Microsoft\Windows\* with no SD are highly suspicious, (3) check binary signature of any executable referenced. Microsoft recommends auditing TaskScheduler/Operational event 4698 and enabling 'Audit Other Object Access Events' to catch future creations. No reboot needed.

findings.verification: @(Get-ChildItem 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Schedule\TaskCache\Tree' -Recurse -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | Where-Object { (Get-ItemProperty -Path $_.PSPath -Name Id -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue) -and -not (Get-ItemProperty -Path $_.PSPath -Name SD -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue) }).Count -eq 0

references.label: Microsoft Security Blog - Tarrask malware
Qualys - Defending against scheduled task attacks
SigmaHQ rule - Removal of SD value to hide schedule task

findings.friendlyName: Deny write access to removable drives not protected by BitLocker
findings.description: Without this policy, any USB stick or external drive is writable, so a user (or a malicious shortcut) can exfiltrate sensitive data to unencrypted media that walks out the door. The 'Deny write access to removable drives not protected by BitLocker' policy mounts unencrypted removable drives read-only; only drives encrypted with BitLocker To Go become writable. This is a core data-loss-prevention control in CIS, NIST and STIG benchmarks.
findings.compatNote: Every previously-writable USB stick will mount read-only until the user encrypts it with BitLocker To Go. This is highly visible end-user friction: photo imports, USB-based installers, IoT firmware sticks, and most consumer-grade flash drives all stop working for write operations. Pair the policy with end-user comms and BitLocker-To-Go onboarding.

registry.name: RDVDenyWriteAccess
registry.purpose: Deny write access to removable data drives that are not BitLocker-protected (the actual enforcement flag).

registry.name: RDVDenyCrossOrg
registry.purpose: Allow cross-organisation BitLocker-encrypted removable drives to be written to (set 1 only in tightly-controlled enterprises).

implementation.notes: Reboot or 'gpupdate /force' + reconnect of any USB device required for the change to take effect. Rollback by setting RDVDenyWriteAccess=0. The CurrentControlSet variant (HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Policies\Microsoft\FVE\RDVDenyWriteAccess) exists on some images but the Policies hive is the canonical GPO-managed location.

references.label: Microsoft Q&A - Deny Write Access to Removable Drives
Microsoft Tech Community - Enable BitLocker Removable Drive policy
Eleven Forum walk-through (verified registry path)

findings.friendlyName: Disable storage of domain credentials in Credential Manager (DisableDomainCreds)
findings.description: When 'Network access: Do not allow storage of passwords and credentials for network authentication' is unset, Windows Credential Manager caches domain (NTLM) credentials a user has chosen to remember. Any process running as the user can read these via CredEnumerate / mimikatz-style techniques, and the cached blob is a recurring target for credential-theft malware. Setting DisableDomainCreds=1 prevents new credentials from being stored and Credential Manager will return errors when applications try to save them.
findings.compatNote: Network shares prompted with 'Remember my credentials' will no longer remember between reboots; mapped drives saved with stored creds may need re-authentication; some line-of-business apps that call CredWrite() will fail silently. RDP saved-credential workflows and 'sign in with' flows that rely on Credential Manager are affected.

registry.name: DisableDomainCreds
registry.purpose: Maps directly to the 'Network access: Do not allow storage of passwords and credentials for network authentication' security option.

implementation.notes: Reboot required. Existing stored credentials are not deleted - run 'cmdkey /list' and 'cmdkey /delete:<target>' to clear them, or 'rundll32.exe keymgr.dll, KRShowKeyMgr' for the GUI. Rollback: set DisableDomainCreds=0 and reboot.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - Network access: Do not allow storage of passwords and credentials for network authentication
Microsoft Q&A - GPO behaviour and registry mapping

findings.friendlyName: Prohibit simultaneous connections to a non-domain network when on a domain-authenticated network
findings.description: Windows Connection Manager can let a device be connected to two networks at once (e.g. a corporate Ethernet plus a personal phone hotspot). That dual-homing can bridge a hostile network straight onto the trusted LAN and bypass perimeter controls. Enabling 'Prohibit connection to non-domain networks when connected to domain authenticated network' (fBlockNonDomain=1) disables automatic non-domain network connections while a domain network is reachable, eliminating the bridging path.
findings.compatNote: Only meaningful on Active-Directory-joined or hybrid-joined machines. On a non-domain Win11 Pro device this policy has no effect because there is never a 'domain network' to be on. On joined devices, expect Wi-Fi/cellular auto-connect to be suppressed while wired domain connectivity is up; users who legitimately need a hotspot must disconnect the wired link first.

registry.name: fBlockNonDomain
registry.purpose: Enables 'Prohibit connection to non-domain networks when connected to domain authenticated network' (Windows Connection Manager).

registry.name: fMinimizeConnections
registry.purpose: Companion CIS recommendation - minimise the number of simultaneous network connections.

implementation.notes: Effective immediately on next network state change; gpupdate /force and toggling Wi-Fi confirms. Rollback by deleting the values or setting them to 0.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - WindowsConnectionManager Policy CSP (ProhibitConnectionToNonDomainNetworksWhenConnectedToDomainAuthenticatedNetwork)
Microsoft Q&A - Prohibit wired connection to non-domain networks

findings.friendlyName: Disable insecure SMB guest fallback logons (AllowInsecureGuestAuth=0)
findings.description: Windows clients used to fall back to anonymous 'guest' authentication when SMB authentication failed against a remote server. Insecure guest logons disable SMB signing and SMB encryption by definition, leaving the session vulnerable to active man-in-the-middle attacks: a malicious LAN device can spoof the file server, accept the guest fallback and inject arbitrary content into opened files. Microsoft removed insecure guest fallback by default in Windows 10/11 Enterprise, but on Pro it can re-appear when users tinker with NAS settings. Enforcing AllowInsecureGuestAuth=0 hard-blocks the fallback.
findings.compatNote: Many consumer NAS devices, old printers and home routers expose SMB shares with anonymous/guest access only. Once this is enforced, those shares fail to mount with 'You can't access this shared folder because your organization's security policies block unauthenticated guest access'. Fix at the NAS by setting a real username/password.

registry.name: AllowInsecureGuestAuth
registry.purpose: Maps to GPO 'Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Network > Lanman Workstation > Enable insecure guest logons' = Disabled.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - takes effect on next SMB session. Rollback by setting to 1 if a NAS strictly requires guest access (ideally update the NAS instead).

references.label: Microsoft Learn - Enable insecure guest logons in SMB2 and SMB3
Microsoft Tech Community - SMB Signing and Guest Authentication
Microsoft Learn - LanmanWorkstation Policy CSP

findings.friendlyName: Require SMB server-side digital signing (LanmanServer)
findings.description: SMB signing places an HMAC over every SMB message so that an in-path attacker cannot tamper with reads, writes, or relay an authenticated session against another host (NTLM relay / SMB relay attacks). When the server side does not require signatures, an attacker who reaches the network can downgrade clients into unsigned sessions and either modify file content in flight or relay credentials elsewhere. Windows 11 24H2 already requires SMB signing by default; this finding catches older images and explicit overrides where the registry value was set to 0.
findings.compatNote: SMB1 clients (Win9x, very old NAS firmware, some industrial PLCs) cannot sign and will be refused. Domain controllers and modern Windows clients all support signing and are unaffected.

registry.name: RequireSecuritySignature
registry.purpose: Maps to 'Microsoft network server: Digitally sign communications (always)' = Enabled.

registry.name: EnableSecuritySignature
registry.purpose: Maps to 'Microsoft network server: Digitally sign communications (if client agrees)' = Enabled.

implementation.notes: Reboot or restart of LanmanServer service required for clean effect. Rollback: set both to 0. To verify post-change: Get-SmbServerConfiguration | Select RequireSecuritySignature, EnableSecuritySignature.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - Overview of SMB signing
Microsoft Learn - Microsoft network server: Digitally sign communications (always)
Microsoft Learn - Control SMB signing behavior

findings.friendlyName: Require SMB client-side digital signing (LanmanWorkstation)
findings.description: The client-side counterpart to server SMB signing. When the workstation does not require signatures on outgoing SMB traffic, an attacker on the same broadcast domain can mount NTLM/SMB relay attacks (e.g. PetitPotam, ntlmrelayx) by coercing the client into authenticating to a malicious server which then replays the credentials elsewhere. Setting RequireSecuritySignature=1 on the client side ensures the client refuses to talk to an SMB server that doesn't sign, closing the relay path.
findings.compatNote: Cannot connect to legacy SMB1-only servers or NAS devices that don't implement signing. Some printer scan-to-share boxes, very old WD My Cloud devices, and old VMware ESXi datastore exports fall into this bucket. Update the server side or carve out a per-host exception.

registry.name: RequireSecuritySignature
registry.purpose: Maps to 'Microsoft network client: Digitally sign communications (always)' = Enabled.

registry.name: EnableSecuritySignature
registry.purpose: Maps to 'Microsoft network client: Digitally sign communications (if server agrees)' = Enabled.

implementation.notes: Reboot or 'Set-SmbClientConfiguration -RequireSecuritySignature $true -Confirm:$false' to apply live. Rollback: set both to 0.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - Microsoft network client: Digitally sign communications (always)
Microsoft Learn - Overview of SMB signing
Microsoft Troubleshoot - SMB signing

findings.originalName: Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares
findings.friendlyName: Block anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares (RestrictAnonymous + RestrictAnonymousSAM)
findings.description: By default, certain anonymous (null-session) SMB/RPC calls are allowed to enumerate the local SAM database (returning usernames, RIDs, group membership) and share names. Reconnaissance tools (enum4linux, nltest, NetSessionEnum, SAMRi10) use exactly this to map out a target before attacking. Setting RestrictAnonymousSAM=1 blocks SAM enumeration; RestrictAnonymous=1 additionally blocks anonymous enumeration of SAM and shares together. Both registry values map to two separate Security Options of the same name.

registry.name: RestrictAnonymousSAM
registry.purpose: Blocks anonymous enumeration of local SAM accounts. Maps to the security option of the same name.

registry.name: RestrictAnonymous
registry.purpose: Blocks anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares (the broader 'shares' variant of the policy).

implementation.notes: Reboot recommended. Default on Win11 Pro is already RestrictAnonymousSAM=1; the BD finding usually flags the missing-or-zero RestrictAnonymous twin. Rollback: set to 0.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares
Microsoft Learn - Network access: Restrict anonymous access to Named Pipes and Shares
CIS Benchmark - Windows 11 Enterprise (NIST checklist mirror)

findings.friendlyName: Disallow WinRM service from storing RunAs credentials (DisableRunAs=1)
findings.description: The WinRM service can be configured so that PowerShell session configurations or WS-Management plugins run under a stored 'RunAs' account. Those credentials are persisted on disk (LSA secrets) and are an attractive target for credential theft - a compromised local admin can read them and pivot. Setting DisableRunAs=1 prevents new RunAsUser/RunAsPassword values from being saved on any WinRM plugin and rejects calls that try to use one. Maps to GPO 'Disallow WinRM from storing RunAs credentials' = Enabled.
findings.compatNote: On a typical Win11 Pro device WinRM is not actively hosting custom plugins, so enabling this has no functional impact. Only environments that have explicitly configured constrained-endpoint PowerShell remoting with a fixed service account will see breakage - those should re-architect to use group-managed service accounts instead.

registry.name: DisableRunAs
registry.purpose: Hard-disables WinRM RunAs credential storage system-wide via GPO.

command.description: Best-effort cleanup of any pre-existing RunAsUser bindings on built-in PSSession configurations.

implementation.notes: Reboot or 'Restart-Service WinRM' to apply. Rollback: delete the DisableRunAs value.

references.label: STIG - Disallow WinRM from storing RunAs credentials (V-220867)
Microsoft Learn - PowerShell remoting and WinRM security
Microsoft Learn - ADMX_WindowsRemoteManagement Policy CSP

findings.friendlyName: Disable WinRM Digest authentication on both client and service
findings.description: Digest authentication is a legacy challenge-response scheme in WS-Management that has weaker cryptographic guarantees than Kerberos or Negotiate and can be downgraded over MITM-controlled network paths. CIS, STIG and Microsoft Security Baseline all recommend disabling Digest on both the WinRM client (so this host won't send Digest credentials to a remote server) and the WinRM service (so this host won't accept them). Setting AllowDigest=0 in both Client and Service policy hives enforces it via GPO regardless of local 'winrm set' state.

registry.name: AllowDigest
registry.purpose: Maps to 'WinRM Client > Disallow Digest authentication' = Enabled. Stops this host from sending Digest credentials.

registry.name: AllowDigest
registry.purpose: Defence-in-depth: stops this host accepting inbound Digest auth even if a listener is configured.

implementation.notes: Reboot or 'Restart-Service WinRM' to apply. Rollback: set both values to 1.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - RemoteManagement Policy CSP (DisallowDigestAuthentication)
Microsoft Learn - PowerShell remoting and WinRM security
Microsoft Learn - ADMX_WindowsRemoteManagement Policy CSP

findings.friendlyName: Enable PowerShell Script Block Logging (4104 events)
findings.description: PowerShell Script Block Logging records the actual de-obfuscated text of every script block PowerShell parses, written to the Microsoft-Windows-PowerShell/Operational event log as event 4104. It is by far the most useful single defensive telemetry for catching living-off-the-land attacks: Cobalt Strike beacons, fileless droppers, encoded -EncodedCommand payloads and Empire all show up in 4104. The control adds detection capability without changing what scripts are allowed to run.

registry.name: EnableScriptBlockLogging
registry.purpose: Master switch for 4104 event logging.

registry.name: EnableScriptBlockInvocationLogging
registry.purpose: Adds 4105/4106 start/stop events around each invocation - useful for forensic timelines, slightly noisier.

implementation.notes: Effective on next PowerShell process start - no reboot. Consider also enabling Module Logging (HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\PowerShell\ModuleLogging) for full coverage. Rollback: delete the keys or set to 0. The Operational log defaults to ~15MB; bump to >=200MB for high-cardinality endpoints (wevtutil sl Microsoft-Windows-PowerShell/Operational /ms:209715200).

references.label: Microsoft Learn - about_Logging_Windows
Microsoft Learn - WindowsPowerShell Policy CSP (Turn on PowerShell Script Block Logging)

findings.friendlyName: Always show known file extensions in Explorer (HideFileExt=0)
findings.description: By default, Windows Explorer hides extensions for known file types. Phishing payloads exploit this constantly: 'Invoice.pdf.exe' renders as 'Invoice.pdf' with a PDF icon, and the user double-clicks an executable thinking it's a document. Setting HideFileExt=0 makes the real extension visible at all times, dramatically reducing the success rate of double-extension lures. This is a per-user setting under HKCU.

registry.name: HideFileExt
registry.purpose: Show extensions for known file types in Explorer for the current user.

command.description: Restart Explorer so the change is visible immediately. Explorer auto-relaunches via the userinit shell.

implementation.notes: Per-user (HKCU) - apply for every user. For new users: also write the same value to HKU\.DEFAULT\... or load NTUSER.DAT in Default\NTUSER.DAT. No reboot. Rollback: HideFileExt=1.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - Folder Options registry mapping (HideFileExt)
Splunk Detection - Disable Show Hidden Files / HideFileExt abuse

findings.friendlyName: Audit SMB shares granting read access to Everyone (manual review)
findings.description: An SMB share whose share-level ACL contains 'Everyone' (S-1-1-0) with Read access is reachable by any authenticated or unauthenticated network principal that can hit TCP/445. Combined with NTFS ACL inheritance bugs or guest auth fallback, this is a classic data-leak vector and the basis of countless ransomware initial-access traces. There is no safe automatic remediation - 'Everyone:Read' might be exactly what the share is supposed to do (e.g. a public driver staging share). The correct response is to enumerate every share and its ACL, then review each one with the system owner before tightening to a specific group.
findings.compatNote: Read-only enumeration. No system change.

command.command: Get-SmbShare | Where-Object { $_.Name -notin 'ADMIN$','IPC$','print$' -and $_.Name -notmatch '^[A-Z]\$$' } | ForEach-Object { $share = $_; Get-SmbShareAccess -Name $share.Name | Where-Object { $_.AccountName -in 'Everyone','NT AUTHORITY\Anonymous Logon','BUILTIN\Guests' -and $_.AccessRight -in 'Read','Change','Full' } | Select-Object @{n='Share';e={$share.Name}}, @{n='Path';e={$share.Path}}, AccountName, AccessRight, AccessControlType } | Format-Table -AutoSize
command.description: Lists every non-admin share whose share-level ACL grants Everyone (or Anonymous / Guests) any access. Cross-check with NTFS ACL on the underlying path.

command.command: Get-SmbShare | Where-Object { $_.Name -notlike '*$' } | ForEach-Object { $p = $_.Path; if (Test-Path $p) { (Get-Acl $p).Access | Where-Object { $_.IdentityReference -in 'Everyone','NT AUTHORITY\Anonymous Logon' } | Select-Object @{n='Share';e={$_.Name}}, @{n='Path';e={$p}}, IdentityReference, FileSystemRights, AccessControlType } }
command.description: Cross-checks NTFS ACLs of share root paths for Everyone-style entries (share ACL + NTFS ACL together determine effective permission).

implementation.notes: DO NOT auto-remove. Each Everyone entry must be reviewed against intent. Common safe remediation: replace 'Everyone:Read' with 'Authenticated Users:Read' on share ACL, and tighten NTFS to a specific group. To remove via PowerShell once approved: Revoke-SmbShareAccess -Name <ShareName> -AccountName Everyone -Force.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - Get-SmbShareAccess cmdlet
CIS Benchmark - Windows 11 Enterprise (NIST checklist mirror)
Auditing SMB shares with PowerShell

findings.friendlyName: Disable the Windows Remote Management (WS-Management) service (WinRM)
findings.description: WinRM is the WS-Management listener that accepts inbound PowerShell remoting and DCOM-over-HTTP management traffic on TCP/5985 (HTTP) and TCP/5986 (HTTPS). On a stand-alone Win11 Pro workstation it has no legitimate use, yet a running WinRM service is a frequent post-exploitation pivot target (Cobalt Strike's winrm beacon, Evil-WinRM, lateral movement via Invoke-Command). Stopping the service and setting StartupType=Disabled removes the listener, the firewall pin-holes opened by 'Enable-PSRemoting', and the service-account credential surface together. Bitdefender flags any host where WinRM is auto-started.
findings.compatNote: Disabling WinRM breaks any inbound remote PowerShell session (Enter-PSSession, Invoke-Command -ComputerName), Ansible's winrm connection plugin, the WS-Management transport used by Server Manager / RSAT against this host, Windows Admin Center remoting via the WinRM channel, and any DSC pull workflow that targets this host. Outbound PowerShell remoting from this host to others is unaffected because the client uses winhttp.dll, not the local WinRM service.

command.description: Stop the running WinRM service (no error if it was already stopped).
Prevent the service from starting on next boot. Equivalent to 'sc config WinRM start= disabled'.
Defence-in-depth: disable the inbound firewall rules that 'Enable-PSRemoting' would have opened, so the listener is unreachable even if the service is re-enabled accidentally.

implementation.notes: Reboot not strictly required - the service is stopped immediately. Rollback: 'Set-Service -Name WinRM -StartupType Manual; Start-Service WinRM; Enable-NetFirewallRule -DisplayGroup ''Windows Remote Management'''. If you intend to use PowerShell remoting later, prefer Enable-PSRemoting which re-creates the standard listener configuration.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - Enable and disable WinRM
Microsoft Learn - PowerShell remoting and WinRM security
Microsoft Learn - WinRM service troubleshooting
Microsoft Learn - Set-Service cmdlet

findings.friendlyName: Disable the Telephony service (TapiSrv)
findings.description: TapiSrv is the user-mode service host (running inside svchost.exe) for the legacy Telephony API (TAPI 2.x/3.x). It abstracts modems, dial-up, fax devices and TAPI-based softphones. On a modern Windows 11 Pro desktop with no modem/fax hardware, it is dead surface: TapiSrv loads third-party Telephony Service Provider (TSP) DLLs into its address space, which has historically been a privilege-escalation path (a malicious or vulnerable TSP gets to run inside a SYSTEM-context service). Setting StartupType=Disabled removes the service and the load path together.
findings.compatNote: Disabling TapiSrv breaks any TAPI-based softphone (some legacy enterprise PBX clients, certain CRM dialers), fax modems sending/receiving via Windows Fax & Scan, dial-up RAS connections, and a small set of accessibility tools that route audio via TAPI. Modern VoIP clients (Teams, Zoom, Webex, Discord) do NOT use TAPI and are unaffected. Default startup on Win11 Pro is 'Manual' - the service starts on demand; many systems never start it at all, which is why disabling it usually has no observable effect.

command.description: Stop TapiSrv if currently running. Most desktops never start it on demand, so this is usually a no-op.
Prevent the service from being started on demand by any TAPI client.

implementation.notes: No reboot required. Rollback: 'Set-Service -Name TapiSrv -StartupType Manual'. If a downstream app reports a TAPI error after disabling, that app is exactly the legacy dependency you're trying to remove - audit it before re-enabling.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - TAPI Server (TAPISRV)
Microsoft Learn - TAPI Service Provider Overview
Microsoft Learn - TAPI 3.1 Overview (TAPISRV inside svchost)
Microsoft Learn - Set-Service cmdlet

findings.friendlyName: Disable the Smart Cards for Windows service (SCardSvr)
findings.description: SCardSvr (Smart Cards for Windows) is the resource manager service that brokers communication between PC/SC-compliant smart card readers and any application that uses the Winscard.dll API. On a Win11 Pro desktop with no smart card reader, no YubiKey-PIV usage, no CAC/PIV logon and no Windows Hello for Business with a smart card credential provider, the service is unused attack surface. Reducing the surface stops third-party reader drivers and minidrivers from being auto-loaded into the service's address space and removes one less SYSTEM-context auto-started service.
findings.compatNote: Disabling SCardSvr breaks every smart-card-based authentication path: domain logon with a CAC/PIV/HID Crescendo card, RDP with smart-card redirection, certificate-based VPN/Wi-Fi using a card-stored cert, EFS using a card-stored key, code-signing with a HSM-backed cert (Yubico/Nitrokey FIDO devices in CCID mode also stop), and any browser flow that calls CryptoAPI to enumerate card-stored certificates. If you use a YubiKey only as a FIDO2 security key (WebAuthn) it does NOT need this service; if you use it for PIV (smart card) it does. Default startup is 'Manual' on Win11 Pro; only disable if you are certain no smart card usage exists.

command.command: if (-not (Get-PnpDevice -Class SmartCardReader -PresentOnly -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue)) { Stop-Service -Name SCardSvr -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue; Set-Service -Name SCardSvr -StartupType Disabled } else { Write-Warning 'Smart card reader detected - skipping disable. Review SCardSvr manually.' }
command.description: Conservative disable: only stop+disable SCardSvr if no smart card reader is presently enumerated. This avoids accidentally locking out a user who logs in with a CAC/PIV card.

implementation.notes: Reboot not required. Rollback: 'Set-Service -Name SCardSvr -StartupType Manual; Start-Service SCardSvr'. The companion services SCPolicySvc (Smart Card Removal Policy) and ScDeviceEnum (Smart Card Device Enumeration) can be reviewed separately - they have a similar threat model but only matter when a reader is present.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - Smart Cards for Windows Service
Microsoft Learn - How Smart Card Sign-in Works in Windows
Microsoft Learn - Smart Card Technical Reference
Microsoft Learn - Set-Service cmdlet

findings.friendlyName: Deny machine-wide microphone access via the Capability Access Manager (privacy hardening)
findings.description: Bitdefender's 'Microphone Service' finding is a labelling artefact: there is no Windows service named 'Microphone' on Windows 11. The audio service (Audiosrv) is critical and must NOT be disabled - doing so breaks all audio output, system sounds, accessibility narration and most modern apps. The correct mapping is the privacy control: the Capability Access Manager broker uses HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\CapabilityAccessManager\ConsentStore\microphone\Value to determine the machine-wide consent state for app microphone access. Setting it to 'Deny' is exactly what the Settings > Privacy & security > Microphone master toggle does when the administrator turns it off, and it applies to both Store/UWP apps and Win32 desktop apps that participate in the consent broker.
findings.compatNote: Every consent-broker-aware microphone consumer stops capturing: Microsoft Teams, Zoom, Discord, Slack huddles, Cortana voice activation, Voice Recorder, Voice Access, Windows Speech Recognition, browser getUserMedia() audio in Edge/Chrome (when running as MSIX/AppContainer), and any WebRTC client. Some legacy Win32 apps that talk directly to MMDevAPI/WASAPI without the broker (older audio editors, some VoIP softphones) may still bypass it - audit per-app under HKLM:\...\ConsentStore\microphone\NonPackaged\ if you need belt-and-braces coverage. Rollback is one-line and immediate.

registry.name: Value
registry.purpose: Machine-wide microphone consent state. 'Deny' = the master Settings > Privacy > Microphone toggle is OFF. 'Allow' = ON. REG_SZ string, not DWORD.

command.description: Idempotent set: ensures the key exists, then writes the REG_SZ Value=Deny.
Optional audit step: list per-Win32-app microphone consent decisions under the NonPackaged subkey so you can spot any desktop app that previously had Allow.

implementation.notes: Effective on next app launch / next consent check - no reboot. To rollback, set Value back to 'Allow' or delete the value to revert to user-driven consent. DO NOT apply this to Audiosrv: stopping the Windows Audio service (Audiosrv) is destructive and is NOT what this finding asks for.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - Capability Access Manager (CapabilityAccessManager) discussion
Microsoft Learn - Where Windows stores microphone consent (ConsentStore)
Microsoft Learn - Microphone access broken via ConsentStore registry
Microsoft Support - Windows microphone privacy settings

findings.friendlyName: Block IPv6 source-routed packets (Tcpip6 DisableIPSourceRouting=2)
findings.description: IPv6 source routing (Routing Header type 0, 'RH0') lets the sender dictate the path a packet takes through the network, which can be used to bypass ACLs, route packets around a stateful firewall, amplify traffic, or fingerprint internal topology. RFC 5095 deprecated RH0 specifically because of these abuses. The Microsoft hardening setting DisableIPSourceRouting on the IPv6 stack (Tcpip6\Parameters) accepts: 0 = forward all, 1 = do not forward source-routed packets but still process locally, 2 = drop all incoming source-routed packets entirely. Value 2 is the CIS / Microsoft Security Baseline / DISA STIG recommendation. The IPv4 twin (Tcpip\Parameters\DisableIPSourceRouting=2) is usually already set on baselined Windows 11; this finding catches the IPv6-only side that often gets missed.
findings.compatNote: No legitimate modern application relies on IPv6 source routing - every mainstream OS and middlebox dropped or deprecated RH0 a decade ago. Setting the value to 2 is the same default many cloud and enterprise images already ship with.

registry.name: DisableIPSourceRouting
registry.purpose: Drop all incoming IPv6 source-routed packets. 0=allow forwarding, 1=do not forward, 2=drop entirely (recommended).

implementation.notes: Reboot required to take effect (the IPv6 stack reads the value at boot via tcpip6.sys init). Rollback: set to 0 (default 'allow') or remove the value. If you also want to confirm the IPv4 twin is hardened, check HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Tcpip\Parameters\DisableIPSourceRouting.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - DisableIPSourceRouting registry guidance (Q&A)
Microsoft Learn - Disable IP source routing (Q&A)
Microsoft Learn - TCP/IP and NBT configuration parameters
Microsoft Learn - Configure IPv6 for advanced users

findings.friendlyName: Require Windows Hello facial-recognition anti-spoofing (EnhancedAntiSpoofing=1)
findings.description: Without Enhanced Anti-Spoofing, a Windows Hello facial-recognition camera that is below the 'Hello-grade' bar (no near-infrared sensor, or relaxed liveness checks) can in principle be unlocked by a high-quality printed photo, a phone screen showing the user's face, or a 3D-printed mask. Enabling EnhancedAntiSpoofing=1 forces Windows Hello Face to run additional liveness/spoof-detection checks and refuses enrollment / unlock on cameras that don't support them, eliminating non-IR webcams as a Hello credential source. Maps to GPO 'Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Biometrics > Facial Features > Configure enhanced anti-spoofing' = Enabled.
findings.compatNote: On a system with a proper Windows Hello camera (IR + colour sensor, e.g. Surface Pro, ThinkPad with IR webcam, dedicated Hello-certified peripherals), enabling this is transparent and strictly safer. On a system with only a non-IR webcam where a user previously enrolled Hello Face anyway, enrollment / sign-in will be refused and the user must fall back to PIN/password until they get a compliant camera. PIN, fingerprint, and password sign-in are unaffected.

registry.name: EnhancedAntiSpoofing
registry.purpose: Forces Windows Hello Face to require enhanced anti-spoofing/liveness checks. Maps to GPO 'Configure enhanced anti-spoofing' = Enabled.

command.description: Ensure the policy key exists before setting the value (it does not exist by default on most Win11 Pro images).

implementation.notes: Reboot or sign-out/sign-in required for the policy to take effect on the Windows Hello face provider. Rollback: set EnhancedAntiSpoofing=0 or delete the value. The companion fingerprint / iris equivalents (PassportForWork CSP) live elsewhere - this finding scopes only to facial recognition.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - Windows Hello Enhanced Sign-in Security
Microsoft Learn - Windows Hello face authentication
Microsoft Learn - Enhanced Anti-Spoofing for external peripherals (Q&A)
Microsoft Learn - Facial recognition and Group Policy

findings.friendlyName: Restrict Windows Ink Workspace above the lock screen (AllowWindowsInkWorkspace=1)
findings.description: Windows Ink Workspace is reachable from the lock screen by default. That gave an attacker with brief physical access a small set of pre-authentication actions (Snip & Sketch, Sticky Notes, Whiteboard launcher) and historically enabled UI-redress techniques and screen-content leaks via the lock-screen surface. The AllowWindowsInkWorkspace policy supports three values: 0 = workspace fully disabled (most restrictive, breaks pen workflows), 1 = workspace enabled but blocked above the lock screen (the recommended hardening for users who actually use a pen), 2 = enabled everywhere including above the lock screen (default). Setting the value to 1 keeps stylus productivity intact for the signed-in user while removing the lock-screen attack surface.
findings.compatNote: After applying, opening the Ink Workspace from the lock screen is silently suppressed; once the user signs in, pen + Ink Workspace, Snip & Sketch, Whiteboard and Sticky Notes all continue to work normally. On a non-touch / non-pen desktop the change has no observable user impact at all. Set to 0 instead of 1 if you want to fully remove the workspace UI on a kiosk-style device.

registry.name: AllowWindowsInkWorkspace
registry.purpose: 0 = Disabled, ink workspace inaccessible. 1 = Enabled, but disabled above the lock screen (recommended). 2 = Enabled everywhere (default).

command.description: Ensure the policy key exists before writing the value.

implementation.notes: Effective on next sign-in / explorer restart, no reboot required. Rollback: delete the value (defaults back to 2 = unrestricted) or set explicitly to 2. If you also want to suppress the 'Suggested apps' surface, set AllowSuggestedAppsInWindowsInkWorkspace=0 in the same key.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - WindowsInkWorkspace Policy CSP
Microsoft Learn - MDM_Policy_Config01_WindowsInkWorkspace02
Microsoft Learn - Windows Ink workspace registry value (Q&A)
Microsoft Learn - Customize Pen and Windows Ink

findings.friendlyName: Enable Windows LAPS (Local Administrator Password Solution) policy
findings.description: Windows LAPS is built into Windows 11 22H2 and later (and back-ported to Windows 10 / Server via cumulative update). It automatically generates a unique, complex password for the managed local administrator account on each machine, rotates it on a schedule, and backs the current password up to either Microsoft Entra ID or on-prem Active Directory. This stops the classic 'pass-the-hash across the fleet' attack where one local admin password compromise gives an attacker every other machine, and stops the 'helpdesk uses the same builtin Administrator password everywhere' anti-pattern. The policy registry hive HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Policies\LAPS controls all of it. BackupDirectory must be 1 (Entra) or 2 (AD) for actual rotation to occur; on a workgroup-only Win11 Pro Home/SOHO machine LAPS will not have a place to back the password up and the values are inert.
findings.compatNote: LAPS only does its job on a Microsoft Entra-joined or Active-Directory-joined device. On a stand-alone / workgroup Win11 Pro the BackupDirectory=1/2 setting will log warnings and no password rotation will happen because there is no directory to write to - the policy values sit there harmlessly until the device is joined. After enrollment, the managed local administrator account password changes on the configured schedule; any helpdesk runbook that relies on a shared static local admin password must be updated to retrieve the rotated password from Entra/AD. Turning this on without a tested retrieval procedure can lock administrators out of a machine.

registry.name: BackupDirectory
registry.purpose: Where to back up the rotated password. 0 = Disabled, 1 = Microsoft Entra ID, 2 = on-prem Active Directory. Use 2 for AD-joined, 1 for Entra-joined. Set to 0 (or remove) on a stand-alone box.

registry.name: PasswordAgeDays
registry.purpose: Maximum password age before rotation, in days. CIS-recommended baseline is 30; valid range is roughly 1-365.

registry.name: PasswordLength
registry.purpose: Length of the generated password. Range 8-64. 20 is a defensible default that comfortably exceeds NTLM offline-cracking thresholds.

registry.name: PasswordComplexity
registry.purpose: Character set used for generation. 1 = upper, 2 = upper + lower, 3 = upper + lower + digits, 4 = upper + lower + digits + special (recommended).

registry.name: PostAuthenticationActions
registry.purpose: What to do after a managed-account interactive logon completes. 1 = reset password, 3 = reset password + sign out the managed account, 5 = reset + reboot. 3 is the typical CIS-recommended balance.

registry.name: PostAuthenticationResetDelay
registry.purpose: Hours to wait after the managed account authenticates before doing the post-auth action. 0 disables. 24 gives a one-day grace window.

command.description: Ensure the policy key exists before writing values (the key is not present on a fresh image).

command.command: Get-Command -Module LAPS -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | Select-Object Name; Invoke-LapsPolicyProcessing -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
command.description: Confirm the LAPS PowerShell module is present (Win11 22H2+) and force an immediate policy-processing pass instead of waiting for the scheduled refresh.

implementation.notes: Requires Win11 22H2 or later for the in-box Windows LAPS (older 'Microsoft LAPS' MSI on 21H2 uses a different registry hive HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft Services\AdmPwd). For Entra-joined devices, the tenant must first allow LAPS at https://entra.microsoft.com > Devices > Device settings > Enable Microsoft Entra Local Administrator Password Solution. Verify rotation worked: 'Get-LapsADPassword <computer>' (AD) or check the device under Entra > Devices > Local administrator password recovery. Rollback: set BackupDirectory=0 and remove the LAPS policy key.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - Windows LAPS overview
Microsoft Learn - Configure policy settings for Windows LAPS
Microsoft Learn - LAPS CSP
Microsoft Learn - Windows LAPS passwords and passphrases
Microsoft Learn - Windows LAPS troubleshooting guidance

findings.friendlyName: Block users from overriding HTTPS certificate errors in IE / WebOC (PreventIgnoreCertErrors=1)
findings.description: By default the Internet Explorer / WebBrowser-control 'There is a problem with this website's security certificate' interstitial offers a 'Continue to this website (not recommended)' link. A single click bypasses every certificate validation check that the platform just performed - exactly the user step a network attacker counts on during an HTTPS man-in-the-middle. The PreventIgnoreCertErrors policy removes that escape hatch: the override link is suppressed and the user must close the page. The setting is honoured by the IE/Edge IE-Mode pipeline and by every Win32 app that hosts the WebBrowser ActiveX control or WebView1 (HTML help, embedded help in many old line-of-business apps, MMC snap-ins). On a modern Win11 Pro box the legacy IE.exe shell is gone but the underlying mshtml/WebOC stack still ships and is still reachable via IE-Mode, so this hardening is genuinely active surface, not paperwork.
findings.compatNote: Users who legitimately need to reach a server with a broken certificate (self-signed lab gear, expired internal CA) must fix the certificate at the server, install the correct CA into the local Trusted Root store, or use a different browser (modern Chromium-based Edge has its own override flow that this policy does not affect).

registry.name: PreventIgnoreCertErrors
registry.purpose: Machine-wide: removes the 'Continue to this website (not recommended)' override link in IE / WebOC for every user. Maps to GPO 'Prevent ignoring certificate errors' = Enabled (Computer Configuration).

registry.name: PreventIgnoreCertErrors
registry.purpose: Per-user mirror used by IE/WebOC at runtime; set as defence-in-depth because some images read the user hive before the policy hive.

registry.name: PreventIgnoreCertErrors
registry.purpose: Per-user policy variant. Maps to GPO 'Prevent ignoring certificate errors' = Enabled (User Configuration). Belt-and-braces with the HKLM policy.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - takes effect on next IE / WebOC instance. Rollback: delete the values or set them to 0. This setting only governs the override UI; it does not relax certificate validation itself - revoked / expired / mis-issued certs are still rejected, the user just cannot click past the rejection.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - InternetExplorer Policy CSP (PreventIgnoreCertErrors)
STIG Viewer - IE 11 STIG (Prevent ignoring certificate errors)
CIS Microsoft Web Browser Benchmarks

findings.friendlyName: Force IE security zones to read machine settings only (Security_HKLM_only=1)
findings.description: Internet Explorer / Edge IE-Mode / WebOC merges security-zone definitions from HKCU and HKLM at runtime, so a user (or malware running as the user) can override a zone setting that the administrator hardened in HKLM - for example, dropping a phishing site into the Trusted Sites zone or relaxing the Restricted Sites zone to Low for one session. Setting Security_HKLM_only=1 makes the platform ignore every HKCU zone value and use only HKLM, so administrator-defined zones become authoritative and tamper-resistant. CIS, STIG and Microsoft Security Baseline all recommend this for managed endpoints.
findings.compatNote: Once HKLM-only is enforced, any per-user customisation a legitimate user has made (added a self-hosted intranet app to Trusted Sites, lowered the Internet zone for a quirky web app) stops working. Administrators must define the equivalent zone configuration in HKLM (Site to Zone Assignment List, Trusted Sites list) or users will lose access. Default Win11 Pro behaviour without the policy is HKCU-priority, which is exactly the weak posture this finding flags.

registry.name: Security_HKLM_only
registry.purpose: Forces IE / Edge IE-Mode / WebOC to read security-zone settings only from HKLM, ignoring HKCU. Maps to GPO 'Security Zones: Use only machine settings' = Enabled.

implementation.notes: Effective on next IE / WebOC instance; no reboot required. Pair with a managed Site-to-Zone Assignment List (HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Internet Settings\ZoneMap\Domains) so legitimate intranet apps still resolve to Trusted Sites. Rollback: set to 0 or delete the value.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - InternetExplorer Policy CSP (SecurityZonesUseOnlyMachineSettings)
Microsoft Support - IE security zones registry entries for advanced users (KB182569)
Microsoft Tech Community - Managing the Launching Applications and Unsafe Files Setting

findings.friendlyName: Enforce IE warning when certificate Common Name does not match URL (WarnOnBadCertRecving=1)
findings.description: When a server presents a TLS certificate whose Subject / Subject Alternative Name does not match the hostname the user typed, IE / WebOC normally raises the 'The security certificate presented by this website was issued for a different website's address' warning. If WarnOnBadCertRecving is unset or zero, the warning can be suppressed and the user is silently sent to a server that may be impersonating the legitimate one - a textbook MITM symptom. Setting it to 1 forces the warning unconditionally. CIS / DISA STIG / Microsoft baseline all require this. Note: this controls the WARNING, not the actual override - 'Certificate Errors' (PreventIgnoreCertErrors) governs whether the user can click past it.
findings.compatNote: Modern, correctly-issued certificates always have a SAN matching the URL, so on healthy infrastructure this policy is invisible. Internal apps with a CN-only or mis-issued cert will start producing the warning - that is the intended behaviour, fix the cert at the server.

registry.name: WarnonBadCertRecving
registry.purpose: Machine policy form: maps to GPO 'Turn on certificate address mismatch warning' = Enabled (Computer Configuration). The capitalisation 'WarnonBadCertRecving' (lowercase 'on') is the canonical form documented by Microsoft and used by the ADMX template.

registry.name: WarnonBadCertRecving
registry.purpose: User policy mirror. Maps to GPO 'Turn on certificate address mismatch warning' = Enabled (User Configuration).

registry.name: WarnOnBadCertRecving
registry.purpose: Runtime per-user setting (Internet Options > Advanced > 'Warn about certificate address mismatch'). The GPO writes the policy form above; this is the user-facing checkbox state.

implementation.notes: No reboot - effective on next IE/WebOC instance. Rollback: set values to 0 or delete them (the default in modern Win11 Pro is already on, but the policy form pins it so a user cannot uncheck it). Combine with the 'Certificate Errors' finding (PreventIgnoreCertErrors=1) for full coverage: WarnOnBadCertRecving=1 raises the warning, PreventIgnoreCertErrors=1 stops the user clicking past it.

references.label: STIG Viewer - IE 11 STIG (certificate address mismatch warning)
Windows-security.org - Turn on certificate address mismatch warning
Microsoft Q&A - Turn on certificate address mismatch warning

findings.friendlyName: Check Authenticode signatures on programs downloaded via IE / WebOC and refuse invalid ones
findings.description: Two paired settings: (1) CheckExeSignatures = 'yes' tells IE / WebOC to verify the Authenticode signature of every executable it downloads before handing it to the user. (2) RunInvalidSignatures = 0 tells the same pipeline to refuse any download whose signature is missing or fails verification rather than prompt 'Allow software to run or install even if the signature is invalid'. Together they short-circuit a popular phishing technique where the lure delivers an unsigned or signature-tampered installer. The settings affect IE.exe, the Edge IE-Mode session, the WebBrowser ActiveX control and any embedded WebView1 host. CIS IE Benchmark and DISA STIG (DTBI370) both require both values.
findings.compatNote: Internal tools that ship unsigned binaries (build outputs from a developer machine, custom installers, hobby kits) can no longer be downloaded through IE/WebOC and will silently fail. Code-signing the binary is the right fix; for short-term unblocks, pre-stage the file via SMB or a Chromium browser instead. Modern Chromium Edge / Chrome are not affected by these registry values.

registry.name: CheckExeSignatures
registry.purpose: Per-user runtime setting (REG_SZ 'yes'). Mirrors the Internet Options > Advanced > 'Check for signatures on downloaded programs' checkbox.

registry.name: CheckExeSignatures
registry.purpose: Machine-wide runtime mirror so the policy is in effect for every user before HKCU is loaded.

registry.name: RunInvalidSignatures
registry.purpose: Maps to GPO 'Allow software to run or install even if the signature is invalid' = Disabled (User Configuration). 0 = block, 1 = prompt.

registry.name: RunInvalidSignatures
registry.purpose: Maps to the Computer-Configuration form of the same GPO. Belt-and-braces with the HKCU form.

implementation.notes: No reboot. Effective on next IE / WebOC download. Rollback: set RunInvalidSignatures=1 and CheckExeSignatures='no'. Verify the run-time effect by attempting to download a deliberately unsigned binary - it should be refused without a 'Run anyway' prompt.

findings.verification: ((Get-ItemProperty -Path 'HKCU:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Internet Explorer\Download' -Name CheckExeSignatures -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue).CheckExeSignatures -eq 'yes') -and ((Get-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Internet Explorer\Download' -Name RunInvalidSignatures -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue).RunInvalidSignatures -eq 0)

references.label: Tenable - DTBI370-IE11 'Check for signatures on downloaded programs'
Windows-security.org - Allow software to run or install even if the signature is invalid
Microsoft Learn - InternetExplorer Policy CSP

findings.friendlyName: Prevent users from adding or deleting sites in IE security zones (Security_zones_map_edit=0)
findings.description: When this policy is unset, any user (or malicious code running as the user) can open Internet Options > Security > <zone> > Sites and add an attacker-controlled domain to Trusted Sites - which lowers the zone's protection bar to the point of allowing scripted ActiveX, automatic NTLM credential disclosure to that site, and unrestricted file downloads. Setting Security_zones_map_edit=0 disables the Sites button entirely so the zone membership defined by the administrator (HKLM ZoneMap\Domains) is the only one that takes effect. Maps to GPO 'Security Zones: Do not allow users to add/delete sites' = Enabled.
findings.compatNote: Users can no longer self-service add a legitimately-needed internal site to Trusted Sites; they must request it via the administrator-managed Site-to-Zone Assignment List. On a single-user Win11 Pro home device this is friction with no real benefit; on a multi-user / managed device it is a real defence against social-engineering ('please add this URL to Trusted Sites for the form to work') and against malware that quietly modifies HKCU ZoneMap\Domains.

registry.name: Security_zones_map_edit
registry.purpose: Machine policy form. 0 = users cannot add/delete sites (recommended), 1 = users can edit. Maps to GPO Computer Configuration > 'Security Zones: Do not allow users to add/delete sites' = Enabled.

registry.name: Security_zones_map_edit
registry.purpose: User policy mirror. Maps to the same GPO under User Configuration.

implementation.notes: No reboot - effective on next IE / Internet Options launch. Rollback: set to 1 or delete the value. Pair with Security_HKLM_only=1 (the 'Security Zones Only Machine Settings' finding) so HKCU site lists are ignored entirely - belt and braces.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - InternetExplorer Policy CSP (DisableSecurityZonesMapEdit)
Microsoft Support - IE security zones registry entries for advanced users (KB182569)
CIS Microsoft Web Browser Benchmarks

findings.friendlyName: Stop the Windows RSS reader from auto-downloading feed enclosures (DisableEnclosureDownload=1)
findings.description: An RSS / Atom feed item can carry an 'enclosure' - a binary file (commonly a podcast .mp3, but the spec allows any MIME type, including .exe, .msi, .ps1, .docm). The Windows RSS Platform (the engine behind the legacy IE feed reader, several ATOM-aware widgets, and any third-party app that calls Microsoft.FeedsManager) will silently download these enclosures into the user profile in the background, where the file is then a phish-click away from execution. Historically this delivered malware via subscribed feeds and is a textbook 'set-and-forget' attack vector. Setting DisableEnclosureDownload=1 turns auto-download off for every consumer of the RSS Platform on the box. Maps to GPO 'Prevent automatic downloading of enclosures' = Enabled.
findings.compatNote: Users who actively consume podcasts via the legacy Windows RSS reader (very rare on Win11 Pro - most podcast listeners use a dedicated app) lose auto-download; they can still subscribe to feeds and read text content, and they can still manually save individual enclosures. No impact on Edge, Chrome, Outlook RSS, or any third-party reader.

registry.name: DisableEnclosureDownload
registry.purpose: Machine policy: stops the Windows RSS Platform automatically downloading feed enclosures. Maps to GPO Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > RSS Feeds > 'Prevent automatic downloading of enclosures' = Enabled.

registry.name: DisableEnclosureDownload
registry.purpose: User policy mirror. Maps to the same GPO under User Configuration.

implementation.notes: No reboot - takes effect on next feed-sync cycle (default 24h, can force with the Sync Center or by calling FeedsManager.RootFolder.AsyncDownload). Rollback: set to 0 or delete the value. Despite the registry path containing 'Internet Explorer', the setting governs the platform-level RSS engine, not just IE.exe.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - ADMX_RSSFeeds Policy CSP (DisableEnclosureDownload)
Microsoft Learn - Windows RSS Platform overview
CIS Microsoft Web Browser Benchmarks

findings.friendlyName: Prevent users from changing IE security zone policies (Security_options_edit=0)
findings.description: Without this policy, the Custom Level... button in Internet Options > Security lets a user (or a script running as the user) reach into the per-zone URLAction dictionary and change individual settings - e.g. flip 'Run ActiveX controls and plug-ins' from Disable to Enable for the Restricted Sites zone, or relax script execution. The zone definitions ship hardened by default; user-level edits are how that hardening gets unwound. Setting Security_options_edit=0 disables the Custom Level button and locks the slider, so users can no longer change individual URLAction values. Maps to GPO 'Security Zones: Do not allow users to change policies' = Enabled.
findings.compatNote: Users can no longer self-service tune zone settings. Internal apps that legitimately need a relaxed setting (e.g. an old intranet site that requires unsigned ActiveX) must be handled by the administrator setting the zone or specific URLAction in HKLM Policies. On a single-user home machine this is mostly cosmetic; on a managed endpoint it stops 'help me get the form to load' social-engineering attacks where the user is talked through Custom Level... by a malicious caller.

registry.name: Security_options_edit
registry.purpose: Machine policy form. 0 = users cannot change zone policies (recommended), 1 = users can edit. Maps to GPO Computer Configuration > 'Security Zones: Do not allow users to change policies' = Enabled.

registry.name: Security_options_edit
registry.purpose: User policy mirror. Maps to the same GPO under User Configuration.

implementation.notes: No reboot - effective on next Internet Options launch. Rollback: set to 1 or delete. Combine with Security_HKLM_only=1 and Security_zones_map_edit=0 for the full administrator-controlled IE zone posture.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - InternetExplorer Policy CSP (DisableSecuritySettingsCheck)
Microsoft Support - IE security zones registry entries for advanced users (KB182569)
CIS Microsoft Web Browser Benchmarks

findings.friendlyName: Enable Internet Explorer Enhanced Protected Mode (Isolation=PMEM, Isolation64Bit=1)
findings.description: Enhanced Protected Mode (EPM) is the second-generation IE sandbox. It tightens the original Protected Mode by (a) running the content process as an AppContainer, severely limiting registry / file-system reach, (b) on 64-bit Windows, forcing the content process to be 64-bit so ASLR's full entropy benefits apply, and (c) blocking 32-bit ActiveX controls in 64-bit content processes by default. The combined effect is a meaningful uplift for any code path that still hits the IE / WebOC / Edge IE-Mode rendering pipeline. Setting Isolation='PMEM' enables EPM; Isolation64Bit=1 also forces the 64-bit content process. Maps to GPO 'Turn on Enhanced Protected Mode' = Enabled (Computer Configuration) and 'Turn on 64-bit tab processes when running in Enhanced Protected Mode on 64-bit versions of Windows' = Enabled.
findings.compatNote: EPM is what breaks long-tail 32-bit ActiveX controls (Java applets in their original form, very old vendor Web SSO clients, some scanner/printer ActiveX bridges). If a known internal site requires legacy ActiveX, set its zone to Trusted Sites and add the site to the EPM compatibility list rather than disabling EPM globally. Modern websites and modern Edge (Chromium) are unaffected.

registry.name: Isolation
registry.purpose: REG_SZ. 'PMEM' = Enhanced Protected Mode is enabled and the user cannot turn it off. Empty / absent = controlled by the user-facing checkbox in Internet Options > Advanced.

registry.name: Isolation64Bit
registry.purpose: Forces the EPM content process to be 64-bit on x64 Windows. Maps to GPO 'Turn on 64-bit tab processes when running in Enhanced Protected Mode on 64-bit versions of Windows' = Enabled.

implementation.notes: Reboot or full IE / Edge IE-Mode restart required for the kernel to load the AppContainer-isolated content process. Rollback: delete Isolation (or set to empty REG_SZ) to revert to user-controlled, and set Isolation64Bit=0. Verify with: Get-ItemProperty 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Internet Explorer\Main' Isolation,Isolation64Bit. Some Microsoft documentation also references HKCU:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Internet Explorer\Main\Isolation for the user-controlled checkbox state - the Policies hive above takes precedence and is what the GPO writes.

findings.verification: ((Get-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Internet Explorer\Main' -Name Isolation -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue).Isolation -eq 'PMEM') -and ((Get-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Internet Explorer\Main' -Name Isolation64Bit -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue).Isolation64Bit -eq 1)

references.label: Microsoft Learn - InternetExplorer Policy CSP (TurnOnEnhancedProtectedMode)
Microsoft Learn - Enhanced Protected Mode in Internet Explorer (legacy)
ITProToday - Turning on EPM for IE10 and IE11 using Group Policy
CIS Microsoft Web Browser Benchmarks

findings.friendlyName: Stop IE from auto-classifying UNC paths and auto-detected hosts as Local Intranet zone (UNCAsIntranet=0, AutoDetect=0)
findings.description: By default, Internet Explorer / Edge IE-Mode / WebOC treat any UNC path (\\server\share) and any host that 'looks like' it might be an intranet name as members of the Local Intranet zone. The Local Intranet zone has the most relaxed defaults of any IE zone: automatic NTLM credential pass-through, scripted access enabled, ActiveX prompts suppressed. A malicious peer on the LAN that can publish an SMB share, run Responder, or poison LLMNR/NetBIOS responses can therefore harvest the user's NTLMv2 hash automatically the moment a link to \\evil\anything is followed - no warning, no prompt. The DTBI375 STIG and CIS IE Benchmark both require UNCAsIntranet=0 (UNCs are NOT auto-intranet, they go to the Internet zone) and AutoDetect=0 (auto-detect intranet is off). Together these force every UNC and every undeclared host into the more locked-down Internet zone instead.
findings.compatNote: Internal apps that load content via \\server\share UNC URLs in IE / WebOC will start hitting Internet-zone restrictions: scripted ActiveX prompts, no automatic NTLM, mixed-content warnings. The right fix is to declare those servers explicitly in the Site-to-Zone Assignment List (HKLM ZoneMap\Domains > <server> > * = 1 for Intranet) rather than relying on auto-detection. On a stand-alone Win11 Pro home/SOHO box that has no intranet, this policy is pure defence with no functional impact.

registry.name: UNCAsIntranet
registry.purpose: Machine policy: 0 = UNCs (\\server\share) are NOT auto-classified as Local Intranet. Maps to GPO 'Intranet Sites: Include all network paths (UNCs)' = Disabled. STIG ID DTBI375.

registry.name: AutoDetect
registry.purpose: Machine policy: 0 = automatic intranet-network detection is OFF. Maps to GPO 'Turn on automatic detection of the intranet' = Disabled.

registry.name: UNCAsIntranet
registry.purpose: User policy mirror. Some images read HKCU before HKLM at first IE launch.

registry.name: AutoDetect
registry.purpose: User policy mirror of the AutoDetect intranet setting.

implementation.notes: No reboot - effective on next IE / WebOC instance. Rollback: set both to 1 or delete the values. Defence in depth: also enforce SMB signing (parts 1 and the SMB-client / SMB-server signing findings) and disable LLMNR / mDNS so even if a UNC is followed the credential cannot be harvested. The path 'ZoneMap' is the same hive used by Site-to-Zone Assignment List - take care not to delete the parent key when rolling back.

findings.verification: ((Get-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Internet Settings\ZoneMap' -Name UNCAsIntranet -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue).UNCAsIntranet -eq 0) -and ((Get-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Internet Settings\ZoneMap' -Name AutoDetect -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue).AutoDetect -eq 0)

references.label: Tenable - DTBI375-IE11 'All network paths (UNCs) for Intranet sites must be disallowed'
Microsoft Learn - InternetExplorer Policy CSP (IntranetZoneAllowAccessToDataSources / IncludeAllNetworkPaths)
Windows-security.org - Turn on automatic detection of intranet
CIS Microsoft Web Browser Benchmarks

findings.friendlyName: Restrict ActiveX install / execution surface (disable AxInstSV + verify IE Protected Mode is on)
findings.description: Bitdefender's 'ActiveX Protected Mode' finding is ambiguous - the BD knowledge base does not publish a single canonical registry value for it. Cross-referencing CIS / STIG / Microsoft baseline, the underlying intent has two parts that must both be true on a hardened Win11 Pro box: (1) The ActiveX Installer Service (AxInstSV) - which exists specifically to run ActiveX installers as SYSTEM on behalf of standard users when a site is on the AllowList - is disabled. With the service stopped, no ActiveX install can elevate via this path; standard users simply cannot install ActiveX at all. (2) Internet Explorer Protected Mode is enforced for the Internet and Restricted Sites zones, so any ActiveX that does load runs inside the low-integrity / AppContainer sandbox where its blast radius is contained. Both items together are what 'ActiveX is in protected mode' actually means in operational terms. Because the BD finding does not name a single registry value and the right answer depends on whether the device is managed (AllowList of approved install sites) or stand-alone (just disable the service), this is captured as a manual review with a default safe action.
findings.compatNote: Disabling AxInstSV breaks any deployment that depends on the ActiveX Installer Service to elevate installs from an approved-site list - that is rare in 2026 (most enterprises have moved off ActiveX). On a stand-alone Win11 Pro device the service is typically Manual and not running, so disabling it is a no-op for normal users. Forcing IE Protected Mode for the Internet zone (URLAction 2500 = 0 in Zones\3) breaks legacy ActiveX controls that genuinely require Protected Mode off; that is the same compatibility class as the Enhanced Protected Mode finding. Audit before enforcing.

registry.purpose: Per-user URLAction 2500 for the Internet zone (Zone 3). 0 = Protected Mode ON, 3 = Protected Mode OFF. Default is 0 on hardened images; verify rather than overwrite blindly because some apps rewrite this.
Per-user URLAction 2500 for the Restricted Sites zone (Zone 4). 0 = Protected Mode ON. Same caveat: verify before forcibly writing.

command.command: Get-Service -Name AxInstSV -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | Select-Object Name, Status, StartType
command.description: Read-only audit step: report the current state of the ActiveX Installer Service. Default on Win11 Pro is StartType=Manual; if Status=Running, something on the box is actively using ActiveX and needs investigation.

command.command: Stop-Service -Name AxInstSV -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue; Set-Service -Name AxInstSV -StartupType Disabled
command.description: Recommended action on a stand-alone Win11 Pro box: stop and disable AxInstSV so no ActiveX install path can elevate via the service. Skip this on managed machines that rely on the AxInstSV AllowList for legitimate ActiveX deployment.

command.command: (0..4) | ForEach-Object { $z = $_; $p = "HKCU:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Internet Settings\Zones\$z"; $v = (Get-ItemProperty -Path $p -Name '2500' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue).'2500'; [pscustomobject]@{Zone=$z; ProtectedMode = if ($v -eq 0) {'ON'} elseif ($v -eq 3) {'OFF'} else {'(default/unset)'}} } | Format-Table -AutoSize
command.description: Enumerates IE Protected Mode state across all five zones. Zone 0=My Computer, 1=Local Intranet, 2=Trusted Sites, 3=Internet, 4=Restricted Sites. Internet (3) and Restricted (4) MUST report ProtectedMode=ON.

implementation.notes: Manual review: (a) confirm the AxInstSV audit output, then disable as appropriate; (b) confirm the per-zone Protected Mode audit shows ON for zones 3 and 4, flip them to 0 only after confirming no internal ActiveX app needs the relaxed zone. Do NOT blindly write 2500=0 across all zones - some images use other URLAction encodings and you can break Trusted Sites flows. Pair with the 'Enhanced Protected Mode' finding (Isolation=PMEM, Isolation64Bit=1) for the full ActiveX containment story.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - ADMX_ActiveXInstallService Policy CSP
Microsoft Learn - ActiveX installation using Group Policy (IE 11 for IT Pros)
Microsoft Q&A - (Edge) Turn off Protected Mode in IE Mode (Zones\<n>\2500)
Microsoft Tech Community - Internet Properties settings apply not only to IE (Zones\<n>\2500 reference)

findings.friendlyName: Audit SMB shares granting write access to Everyone or BUILTIN\Users (manual review)
findings.description: An SMB share whose share-level ACL contains 'Everyone' (S-1-1-0) or 'BUILTIN\Users' (S-1-5-32-545) with Change or Full Control rights is writable by any authenticated network principal that can hit TCP/445, and - if AllowInsecureGuestAuth is left at its default on consumer NAS gear - by completely unauthenticated peers. That is the canonical ransomware initial-access pivot: an attacker who lands on any LAN host enumerates shares, finds a writable one, and uses it as a staging area for lateral movement (drops a malicious shortcut, an .exe in a folder users browse to, an Office macro, or a poisoned ICA / RDP file). Unlike the Read-only twin (part 1's smb-shares-everyone-read-audit), Everyone:Write effectively never has a legitimate use case on a Win11 Pro endpoint - if a directory genuinely needs to be a public drop box, it should grant Write to a specific group, not Everyone. There is still no safe automatic remediation - blindly stripping Everyone:Write can break a legitimate scan-to-share / drop-box workflow - so the correct response is to enumerate every share with its share-level and NTFS ACL, review each one with the system owner, and then revoke.
findings.compatNote: Read-only enumeration. No system change. Any revoke must be done manually after triage.

command.command: Get-SmbShare | Where-Object { $_.Name -notin 'ADMIN$','IPC$','print$' -and $_.Name -notmatch '^[A-Z]\$$' } | ForEach-Object { $share = $_; Get-SmbShareAccess -Name $share.Name | Where-Object { $_.AccountName -in 'Everyone','NT AUTHORITY\Anonymous Logon','BUILTIN\Users','BUILTIN\Guests' -and $_.AccessRight -in 'Full','Change' -and $_.AccessControlType -eq 'Allow' } | Select-Object @{n='Share';e={$share.Name}}, @{n='Path';e={$share.Path}}, AccountName, AccessRight, AccessControlType } | Format-Table -AutoSize
command.description: Lists every non-admin share whose share-level ACL grants Everyone, Anonymous, BUILTIN\Users or Guests write-equivalent access (Full or Change). The exact query Bitdefender's manual-audit guidance recommends, with Anonymous and Guests added defensively.

command.command: Get-SmbShare | Where-Object { $_.Name -notlike '*$' } | ForEach-Object { $name = $_.Name; $p = $_.Path; if (Test-Path $p) { (Get-Acl $p).Access | Where-Object { ($_.IdentityReference -in 'Everyone','NT AUTHORITY\Anonymous Logon','BUILTIN\Users') -and ($_.FileSystemRights -match 'Write|Modify|FullControl') -and ($_.AccessControlType -eq 'Allow') } | Select-Object @{n='Share';e={$name}}, @{n='Path';e={$p}}, IdentityReference, FileSystemRights, AccessControlType } }
command.description: Cross-checks NTFS ACLs for the underlying directory of each share - effective permission is min(share ACL, NTFS ACL), so a share with Everyone:Full is harmless if NTFS denies write, and a share with Everyone:Read is dangerous if NTFS grants Modify.

command.command: Get-SmbServerConfiguration | Select-Object EnableSMB1Protocol, RequireSecuritySignature, AutoShareWorkstation, AutoShareServer
command.description: Companion sanity check: SMB1 disabled, signing required (see part 1 smb-server-signing-required), and the admin C$/D$/etc. AutoShare* values are not unexpectedly off (which would indicate someone has been tampering with the share infrastructure).

implementation.notes: DO NOT auto-revoke. Each Everyone:Write entry must be reviewed against intent. Standard remediation once approved: Revoke-SmbShareAccess -Name <ShareName> -AccountName Everyone -Force; then Grant-SmbShareAccess -Name <ShareName> -AccountName <SpecificGroup> -AccessRight Change -Force. For shares that are genuinely a public drop box, consider granting Authenticated Users:Change on the share ACL but tightening NTFS to Authenticated Users:Write (no Read), so an attacker who drops a payload cannot also read other users' drops. Pair with smb-disable-insecure-guest-logon (part 1) and smb-server-signing-required so an attacker on the LAN cannot bypass authentication entirely.

findings.verification: @(Get-SmbShare | Where-Object { $_.Name -notmatch '^([A-Z]\$|ADMIN\$|IPC\$|print\$)$' } | ForEach-Object { Get-SmbShareAccess -Name $_.Name } | Where-Object { $_.AccountName -in 'Everyone','NT AUTHORITY\Anonymous Logon','BUILTIN\Users','BUILTIN\Guests' -and $_.AccessRight -in 'Full','Change' -and $_.AccessControlType -eq 'Allow' }).Count -eq 0

references.label: Microsoft Learn - Get-SmbShareAccess cmdlet
Microsoft Learn - Revoke-SmbShareAccess cmdlet
CIS Benchmark - Windows 11 Enterprise (NIST checklist mirror)
Microsoft Learn - SMB security best practices

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer: Java permissions (Local Machine Zone)
findings.friendlyName: Disable Java permissions for the Local Machine Zone (Zone 0, URLAction 1C00 = 0)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 1C00 (Java permissions) for Zone 0, the Local Machine Zone, which is the security context applied to local file:// content (HTML files on disk, .chm help files, mshtml hosts opened with a local path, .hta files in some configurations). When the zone is left at its default 'Low safety' or 'Custom' Java setting, a malicious local HTML payload dropped on disk can launch a Java applet under the most permissive sandbox available - exactly the kind of escalation a local-priv-esc chain wants. Setting this to 0 = 'Disable Java' kills applet startup for any local-machine-rendered HTML. On a Win11 Pro device that is not running IE.exe (because IE.exe is retired) the practical attack surface is small: the Local Machine Zone is reached today only when an old Win32 app embeds the WebBrowser/WebOC control, opens a local HTML file, and that HTML embeds a Java applet via the Java plug-in. That combination is rare in 2025 because Oracle Java's NPAPI plug-in is dead and IE itself no longer launches the Java plug-in on most builds. It still matters for legacy LOB apps on Edge IE-Mode that render local HTML training material, embedded help, or .hta-equivalent files, where this acts as defence-in-depth against an attacker who plants a Java applet next to a benign-looking HTML file.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox unaffected. Zone 0 (Local Machine) hardening can BREAK local HTML pages and CHM help files that legitimately rely on a Java applet to render content - a small population of legacy training packages and embedded help shipped with old industrial / engineering software. If you discover a local document depending on an applet, the right fix is to migrate the content; keeping Java enabled in the Local Machine Zone is not a sustainable security position in 2025.

registry.name: 1C00
registry.purpose: URLAction 1C00 = Java permissions. Value 0 = 'Disable Java' for the Local Machine Zone. Maps to GPO 'Local Machine Zone > Java permissions' = Disable Java.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - effective on next IE / WebOC instance. Rollback: delete the value, or set to 0x00010000 (High safety), 0x00020000 (Medium), 0x00030000 (Low), 0x00800000 (Custom). The machine policy hive (HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\...) is the canonical GPO-managed location; the runtime mirror under HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Internet Settings\Zones\0 is followed when the policy hive is absent. Test with a representative local HTML asset and any Win32 app that embeds WebBrowser before rolling out broadly.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - Internet Explorer security zones registry entries (URLAction reference)
CIS Microsoft Internet Explorer 11 Benchmark
STIG Viewer - Microsoft Internet Explorer 11 STIG

findings.friendlyName: Disable Java permissions for the Local Intranet Zone (Zone 1, URLAction 1C00 = 0)
