findings.description: Controls URLAction 1C00 (Java permissions) for Zone 1, the Local Intranet Zone, which is automatically applied to UNC paths, sites that bypass the proxy, and short-name hostnames. Without this hardening the zone allows Java applets to load with the zone's permission template, which historically meant 'Low safety' on Local Intranet. Java applets in the browser have been an apex exploitation surface since the 2010s (sandbox escapes, deserialization bugs, signed-jar abuse) and the Local Intranet Zone is the one users routinely visit without thinking. Setting URLAction 1C00 = 0 disables Java applet launch for any page rendered in the Intranet Zone. On a modern Win11 Pro device this matters far less than it did pre-2018 because: (a) IE.exe is retired, (b) Oracle's NPAPI plug-in is dead, (c) most internal apps have moved to web SPAs. Where it still matters is Edge IE-Mode rendering of legacy SharePoint / engineering / financial intranet sites that embed an applet for charts, signing, or PDF rendering.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox unaffected. May break legacy intranet sites that rely on Java applets - this is the single most likely real-world breakage of any setting in this catalogue tier, because some industries (manufacturing, banking, engineering) still ship intranet apps with applets. Inventory IE-Mode site-list entries first and test before broad deployment.

registry.name: 1C00
registry.purpose: URLAction 1C00 = Java permissions. Value 0 = 'Disable Java' for the Local Intranet Zone. Maps to GPO 'Intranet Zone > Java permissions' = Disable Java.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - effective on next IE / WebOC instance. Rollback: delete the value or set to a non-zero permission template (0x00010000=High, 0x00020000=Medium, 0x00030000=Low, 0x00800000=Custom). If a legacy intranet applet is critical, prefer carving an exception via the Site-to-Zone Assignment List (move the specific app into a less-restricted zone) over disabling this catalogue item entirely.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - Internet Explorer security zones registry entries (URLAction reference)
CIS Microsoft Internet Explorer 11 Benchmark
STIG Viewer - Microsoft Internet Explorer 11 STIG

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer: Java permissions (Trusted Sites Zone)
findings.friendlyName: Disable Java permissions for the Trusted Sites Zone (Zone 2, URLAction 1C00 = 0)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 1C00 (Java permissions) for Zone 2, the Trusted Sites Zone. The Trusted Sites Zone exists specifically so administrators can grant elevated browser privileges to a curated allow-list - which is exactly why an attacker who can poison that allow-list (via a registry tamper, a phishing 'add this URL to Trusted Sites' lure, or a misconfigured Site-to-Zone Assignment List) wants Java enabled there. With URLAction 1C00 = 0 the zone refuses Java applet launch even for explicitly-trusted hosts; an attacker who gets onto the Trusted list still cannot land an applet-based exploit. On Win11 Pro without IE.exe the Trusted Sites zone only flows through Edge IE-Mode and embedded WebBrowser controls, but Trusted Sites is precisely the zone IE-Mode site-list entries land in, so this hardening has more practical reach than the Local Machine variant.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox unaffected. Any internal app explicitly added to Trusted Sites that depends on a Java applet will stop loading the applet. Inventory IE-Mode site-list entries first; if a Trusted-Sites app legitimately requires Java, the fix is to migrate the app rather than re-enable Java in the broadest-trust IE zone.

registry.name: 1C00
registry.purpose: URLAction 1C00 = Java permissions. Value 0 = 'Disable Java' for the Trusted Sites Zone. Maps to GPO 'Trusted Sites Zone > Java permissions' = Disable Java.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - effective on next IE / WebOC instance. Rollback: delete the value or set to a non-zero permission template (0x00010000=High, 0x00020000=Medium, 0x00030000=Low, 0x00800000=Custom). Combine with the 'ie-security-zones-hklm-only' finding (Security_HKLM_only=1) so a user cannot simply move an applet-bearing host into Trusted Sites via HKCU.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - Internet Explorer security zones registry entries (URLAction reference)
CIS Microsoft Internet Explorer 11 Benchmark
STIG Viewer - Microsoft Internet Explorer 11 STIG

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer: Turn on Protected Mode (Internet Zone)
findings.friendlyName: Force Protected Mode ON for the Internet Zone (Zone 3, URLAction 2500 = 0)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 2500 (Turn on Protected Mode) for Zone 3, the Internet Zone. Protected Mode is IE's mandatory integrity level (MIC) sandbox: tabs render at Low integrity so a successful exploit of mshtml or an embedded ActiveX control cannot write to user-profile or system locations without first defeating the broker process. With URLAction 2500 unset (or set to 3 = OFF), Internet-zone pages render at the user's normal Medium integrity and any RCE inside the renderer immediately has the user's full token. Setting URLAction 2500 = 0 forces Protected Mode ON for every Internet-zone page. On Win11 Pro this is reached only through Edge IE-Mode tabs and through Win32 apps that embed the WebBrowser control, but for those code paths it is one of the cheapest, highest-leverage IE hardenings: it does not change what content can render, it only sandboxes how it renders.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox unaffected. Protected Mode has been the default for the Internet Zone since IE7 / Windows Vista, so enforcing it almost never breaks anything. The one historical edge case is poorly-written ActiveX controls that try to write to HKLM or to Program Files at runtime - those break the same way they would on any default Win11 box.

registry.purpose: URLAction 2500 = Turn on Protected Mode. Value 0 = Protected Mode ON, 3 = OFF. Maps to GPO 'Internet Zone > Turn on Protected Mode' = Enabled.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - takes effect on next IE / WebOC instance. Verify with the 'Tools > Internet options > Security' dialog showing 'Enable Protected Mode' ticked and greyed out (policy-pinned). Rollback: set value to 3 to disable, or delete to revert to the (already-secure) IE7+ default.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - Internet Explorer security zones registry entries (URLAction reference)
CIS Microsoft Internet Explorer 11 Benchmark
STIG Viewer - Microsoft Internet Explorer 11 STIG (Protected Mode)

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer: Turn on Protected Mode (Restricted Sites Zone)
findings.friendlyName: Force Protected Mode ON for the Restricted Sites Zone (Zone 4, URLAction 2500 = 0)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 2500 (Turn on Protected Mode) for Zone 4, the Restricted Sites Zone. The Restricted Sites Zone is where administrators (and the SmartScreen / Outlook attachment hand-off) place known-bad or untrusted sources, including any URL Outlook flags as 'this came from the internet' before render. If Protected Mode is OFF for that zone, exactly the content that is most likely to be malicious renders at full user integrity. Setting URLAction 2500 = 0 forces Protected Mode ON in the Restricted Sites Zone, sandboxing the renderer for the riskiest content the user encounters. The higher BD risk score versus the Internet-zone variant of this finding (77 vs 53) reflects that mismatch. On Win11 Pro the practical reach is Edge IE-Mode and any Win32 app that hands a Restricted-zone URL to WebOC.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox unaffected. Restricted Sites is by definition the zone for content the user does not trust, so sandboxing it harder cannot reasonably be considered breaking. No real-world compatibility footprint.

registry.purpose: URLAction 2500 = Turn on Protected Mode. Value 0 = Protected Mode ON, 3 = OFF. Maps to GPO 'Restricted Sites Zone > Turn on Protected Mode' = Enabled.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - takes effect on next IE / WebOC instance. Rollback: set to 3 or delete the value. Combine with 'ie-zone-restricted-allow-scriptlets', 'ie-zone-restricted-allow-xaml', etc., to keep the Restricted Sites Zone uniformly locked down.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - Internet Explorer security zones registry entries (URLAction reference)
CIS Microsoft Internet Explorer 11 Benchmark
STIG Viewer - Microsoft Internet Explorer 11 STIG (Protected Mode)

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer: Turn on Cross-Site Scripting Filter (Internet Zone)
findings.friendlyName: Enable the IE Cross-Site Scripting Filter for the Internet Zone (Zone 3, URLAction 1409 = 0)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 1409 (Cross-Site Scripting Filter) for Zone 3, the Internet Zone. The XSS Filter is IE's reflected-XSS heuristic: when the renderer detects that a script in the response body looks like content from the request URL or POST body, it neuters the matching tokens. It is a partial mitigation, not a complete defence (modern browsers replaced it with strict CSP and Trusted Types), but it does block a meaningful fraction of opportunistic reflected-XSS payloads against IE-rendered pages. With URLAction 1409 = 3 (Disable) the filter is OFF and any reflected-XSS bug on an Internet-zone site is fully exploitable inside IE-Mode. Setting it to 0 enables the filter. Note that Microsoft itself eventually removed this filter from Edge (Chromium) because of XS-Leaks side-channels - on a Win11 Pro device the only place this URLAction is honoured is IE.exe and the IE-Mode rendering pipeline.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox do not have this filter at all. The filter is conservative and very rarely breaks legitimate pages; the historical XS-Leak concerns it created applied to Edge Chromium, not the IE pipeline being hardened here.

registry.purpose: URLAction 1409 = Turn on Cross-Site Scripting Filter. Value 0 = Enable XSS filter, 3 = Disable. Maps to GPO 'Internet Zone > Turn on Cross-Site Scripting Filter' = Enabled.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - takes effect on next IE / WebOC instance. Rollback: set to 3 or delete the value.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - Internet Explorer security zones registry entries (URLAction reference)
CIS Microsoft Internet Explorer 11 Benchmark
STIG Viewer - Microsoft Internet Explorer 11 STIG

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer: Turn on Cross-Site Scripting Filter (Restricted Sites Zone)
findings.friendlyName: Enable the IE Cross-Site Scripting Filter for the Restricted Sites Zone (Zone 4, URLAction 1409 = 0)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 1409 (Cross-Site Scripting Filter) for Zone 4, the Restricted Sites Zone - the bucket where Outlook-attachment HTML and admin-flagged untrusted sites land. With the filter disabled in this zone, a reflected-XSS payload embedded in an emailed or otherwise restricted HTML document gets to execute fully inside the IE / WebOC renderer. Setting URLAction 1409 = 0 enables the heuristic neutering of reflected scripts. The control is the same engine as the Internet-zone variant; it is listed separately because URLAction is per-zone and Bitdefender flags both. On Win11 Pro the practical reach is Edge IE-Mode and Outlook's HTML-message rendering host (which uses Restricted Sites Zone settings).
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox do not have this filter. Restricted Sites is the riskiest-content zone, so enabling a defensive filter in it has effectively no compatibility cost.

registry.purpose: URLAction 1409 = Turn on Cross-Site Scripting Filter. Value 0 = Enable XSS filter, 3 = Disable. Maps to GPO 'Restricted Sites Zone > Turn on Cross-Site Scripting Filter' = Enabled.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - takes effect on next IE / WebOC instance. Rollback: set to 3 or delete the value.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - Internet Explorer security zones registry entries (URLAction reference)
CIS Microsoft Internet Explorer 11 Benchmark
STIG Viewer - Microsoft Internet Explorer 11 STIG

findings.friendlyName: Disable userData persistence for the Internet Zone (Zone 3, URLAction 1606 = 3)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 1606 (Userdata persistence) for Zone 3, the Internet Zone. userData is a legacy IE-only client storage mechanism (predating localStorage) that lets a page persist up to 128 KB of XML on disk under the user profile, keyed by URL. With persistence enabled in the Internet Zone, an arbitrary site can write a long-lived super-cookie that survives normal cookie clearing, and content already on disk can be read back across sessions to track or fingerprint the user. The mechanism has also been used to exfiltrate previously-cached strings via cross-zone tag-injection. Setting URLAction 1606 = 3 disables userData persistence for Internet-zone pages. On Win11 Pro the practical reach is Edge IE-Mode plus any embedded WebBrowser control - the storage path is %LOCALAPPDATA%\Microsoft\Internet Explorer\UserData. Modern web apps use localStorage / IndexedDB, neither of which is gated by this URLAction, so the breakage surface is essentially zero.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox use localStorage / IndexedDB instead and are unaffected. The userData behaviour was IE-only since IE5 and is not used by any non-legacy site in 2025.

registry.purpose: URLAction 1606 = Userdata persistence. Value 3 = Disable, 0 = Enable. Maps to GPO 'Internet Zone > Userdata persistence' = Disabled.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - takes effect on next IE / WebOC instance. Rollback: set to 0 or delete the value. Existing userData files on disk are not removed by this policy; clear them manually with 'rd /s /q %LOCALAPPDATA%\Microsoft\Internet Explorer\UserData' if needed.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - Internet Explorer security zones registry entries (URLAction reference)
CIS Microsoft Internet Explorer 11 Benchmark
STIG Viewer - Microsoft Internet Explorer 11 STIG

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer: Include local path when user is uploading files to a server (Restricted Sites Zone)
findings.friendlyName: Strip local file path on uploads from the Restricted Sites Zone (Zone 4, URLAction 160A = 3)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 160A (Include local directory path when uploading files to a server) for Zone 4, the Restricted Sites Zone. When this is enabled (value 0), a file <input type=file> upload from a Restricted-zone page sends the full local path (e.g. C:\Users\bob\Documents\Personal\foo.docx) along with the file content. That gives an attacker free username enumeration, profile-path disclosure, and structural reconnaissance of the user's filesystem - all from a page the system already considers untrusted. Setting URLAction 160A = 3 (Disable) strips the path so only the bare filename is transmitted, matching modern browser behaviour. The risk is higher in the Restricted Sites Zone (BD score 75) than Internet (BD 51) precisely because Restricted is the bucket for content the user has been told not to trust.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox already strip the local path unconditionally. The only servers that ever consumed the leaked path were old internal upload tools that displayed it back as a 'thanks for uploading from <path>' message - a non-functional cosmetic dependency.

registry.name: 160A
registry.purpose: URLAction 160A = Include local directory path when uploading files to a server. Value 3 = Disable (do not include path - hardening), 0 = Enable. Maps to GPO 'Restricted Sites Zone > Include local directory path when uploading files to a server' = Disabled.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - takes effect on next IE / WebOC instance. Rollback: set to 0 or delete the value.

references.label: Microsoft Learn - Internet Explorer security zones registry entries (URLAction reference)
CIS Microsoft Internet Explorer 11 Benchmark
STIG Viewer - Microsoft Internet Explorer 11 STIG

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer: Include local path when user is uploading files to a server (Internet Zone)
findings.friendlyName: Strip local file path on uploads from the Internet Zone (Zone 3, URLAction 160A = 3)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 160A (Include local directory path when uploading files to a server) for Zone 3, the Internet Zone. Same mechanism as the Restricted-Sites variant: when enabled (value 0), a <input type=file> upload to an Internet-zone server transmits the full local path of the file alongside its content, leaking the username and folder structure of the user's profile to any third-party site the user uploads to. Setting URLAction 160A = 3 (Disable) sends only the bare filename. The BD score is lower (51) than the Restricted variant (75) because the Internet Zone has more legitimate need to upload to third parties, but the leak is identical and the fix is identical. Modern Chromium-based browsers and Firefox have stripped the path unconditionally for over a decade.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox already strip the local path. No real-world server depends on receiving the path; HTTP multipart POST has carried the bare filename for two decades on every other browser.

registry.name: 160A
registry.purpose: URLAction 160A = Include local directory path when uploading files to a server. Value 3 = Disable, 0 = Enable. Maps to GPO 'Internet Zone > Include local directory path when uploading files to a server' = Disabled.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - takes effect on next IE / WebOC instance. Rollback: set to 0 or delete the value.

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer: Download signed ActiveX controls (Internet Zone)
findings.friendlyName: Disable download of signed ActiveX controls from the Internet Zone (Zone 3, URLAction 1001 = 3)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 1001 (Download signed ActiveX controls) for Zone 3, the Internet Zone. With the default 'Prompt' (value 1) or 'Enable' (value 0), an Internet-zone page can offer a code-signed ActiveX cab/ocx for the user to install - which historically meant 'click Yes and now an attacker has user-mode persistence via an installed BHO, toolbar or COM server'. The fact that a control is signed only proves the publisher's identity at signing time; it does not prove the publisher is benign or that the binary has not been weaponised after signing. Setting URLAction 1001 = 3 (Disable) blocks the download outright for Internet-zone pages, so signed-but-malicious ActiveX cannot be delivered through ordinary web browsing. Trusted-Sites Zone is left at value 0 (Enable) for legitimate enterprise distribution; this finding is specifically the Internet-zone half. On Win11 Pro the practical reach is Edge IE-Mode and embedded WebOC, where ActiveX hosting is still alive.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox do not host ActiveX at all. May break legacy intranet or vendor-portal sites that distribute an ActiveX control through an Internet-zone URL (rather than a properly-zoned Trusted Sites entry). The right fix is to add such vendor-portal hostnames to the Site-to-Zone Assignment List as Trusted Sites, not to relax this Internet-zone setting.

registry.purpose: URLAction 1001 = Download signed ActiveX controls. Value 0 = Enable, 1 = Prompt, 3 = Disable. Setting 3 for the Internet Zone is the CIS / STIG hardening (Trusted-Sites Zone is left at 0).

implementation.notes: No reboot required - takes effect on next IE / WebOC instance. Rollback: set to 1 (Prompt) or 0 (Enable). Note this only governs the IE-Mode 'install this signed control' flow; existing installed ActiveX controls on disk continue to load.

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer: Allow only approved domains to use the TDC ActiveX control (Restricted Sites Zone)
findings.friendlyName: Restrict the TDC ActiveX control to approved domains only in the Restricted Sites Zone (Zone 4, URLAction 120C = 0x10000)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 120C (Allow only approved domains to use the TDC ActiveX control) for Zone 4, the Restricted Sites Zone. The Tabular Data Control (TDC, CLSID {333C7BC4-460F-11D0-BC04-0080C7055A83}) is a legacy IE ActiveX control that loads a delimited text file from a URL into a queryable data island. CVE-2010-0805 and related research demonstrated that the TDC control could be coerced into reading cross-domain data, effectively turning it into a same-origin-policy bypass. Setting URLAction 120C = 0x10000 (decimal 65536, 'administrator approved only') makes the platform refuse to instantiate TDC unless the calling site appears in the Approved-ActiveX list maintained by GPO. This is hardening, not a feature deletion - approved internal apps can still use TDC if explicitly listed. Restricted Sites is the riskiest zone, so getting this right there is the highest priority of the two TDC findings.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox do not host the TDC control. TDC has been effectively defunct for over a decade; restricting it in the Restricted Sites Zone has no real compatibility impact.

registry.name: 120C
registry.purpose: URLAction 120C = Allow only approved domains to use the TDC ActiveX control. Value 0x00010000 (65536) = administrator-approved-only (CIS / STIG hardening), 0 = Allow, 3 = Disable. Maps to GPO 'Restricted Sites Zone > Allow only approved domains to use the TDC ActiveX control' = Enabled.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - takes effect on next IE / WebOC instance. Rollback: set to 0 (allow without approval) or 3 (disable entirely). The 'administrator-approved' list is HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Internet Settings\AllowedControls.

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer: Allow only approved domains to use the TDC ActiveX control (Internet Zone)
findings.friendlyName: Restrict the TDC ActiveX control to approved domains only in the Internet Zone (Zone 3, URLAction 120C = 0x10000)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 120C (Allow only approved domains to use the TDC ActiveX control) for Zone 3, the Internet Zone. Same control as the Restricted-Sites variant: the legacy Tabular Data Control historically allowed cross-domain data reads (CVE-2010-0805 and friends), so any Internet-zone page that can instantiate TDC can be turned into an SOP-bypass primitive. Setting URLAction 120C = 0x10000 (decimal 65536, 'administrator approved only') restricts TDC to sites explicitly listed in the Approved-ActiveX policy. CIS, DISA STIG and Microsoft Security Baseline all set this for both Internet and Restricted zones. On a Win11 Pro device the only place TDC can still be instantiated is Edge IE-Mode and embedded WebOC; modern browsers ignore the control entirely.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox do not host the TDC control. TDC is essentially defunct; legitimate Internet-zone usage in 2025 is vanishingly rare.

registry.name: 120C
registry.purpose: URLAction 120C = Allow only approved domains to use the TDC ActiveX control. Value 0x00010000 (65536) = administrator-approved-only (hardening), 0 = Allow, 3 = Disable. Maps to GPO 'Internet Zone > Allow only approved domains to use the TDC ActiveX control' = Enabled.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - takes effect on next IE / WebOC instance. Rollback: set to 0 or 3. Pair with the Restricted-Sites variant ('ie-zone-restricted-tdc-activex-approved-only') for full coverage.

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer: Allow loading of XAML files (Internet Zone)
findings.friendlyName: Disable loading of XAML files from the Internet Zone (Zone 3, URLAction 2402 = 3)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 2402 (Allow loading of XAML files) for Zone 3, the Internet Zone. XAML browser applications (XBAPs / 'loose XAML') are a WPF-era feature that lets a webpage render a XAML file directly inside the IE host using PresentationHost.exe. The XAML/XBAP attack surface is a recurring source of CVEs (CVE-2007-0042, CVE-2008-2540, CVE-2014-4123, CVE-2017-0070 among others) because PresentationHost.exe runs full WPF / .NET inside the browser context. Setting URLAction 2402 = 3 (Disable) blocks any Internet-zone page from initiating an XBAP / loose-XAML load. Modern Chromium-based browsers do not host PresentationHost.exe at all, so removing the IE entry point closes the only reachable path on Win11 Pro that is not already gone.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox do not host XBAPs. Microsoft itself deprecated XBAP in .NET 5+; loose XAML in the browser is effectively a dead technology. No realistic Internet-zone site depends on this in 2025.

registry.purpose: URLAction 2402 = Allow loading of XAML files. Value 3 = Disable XAML loading (hardening), 0 = Enable, 1 = Prompt. Maps to GPO 'Internet Zone > Allow loading of XAML files' = Disabled.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - takes effect on next IE / WebOC instance. Rollback: set to 0 (Enable) or 1 (Prompt).

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer: Allow loading of XAML files (Restricted Sites Zone)
findings.friendlyName: Disable loading of XAML files from the Restricted Sites Zone (Zone 4, URLAction 2402 = 3)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 2402 (Allow loading of XAML files) for Zone 4, the Restricted Sites Zone. Same control as the Internet-zone variant - XBAPs / loose-XAML hosted by PresentationHost.exe expose the full WPF / .NET runtime to web content and have a long history of RCE CVEs. The Restricted Sites Zone is by definition the bucket for content that has already been flagged untrusted (HTML email rendering, admin-flagged sites). Allowing XAML to load from such content is exactly the worst case. Setting URLAction 2402 = 3 (Disable) closes the path. CIS, STIG and Microsoft Security Baseline all require this for both Internet and Restricted zones.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox do not host XBAPs. Restricted Sites is the riskiest-content zone and XBAP / loose-XAML is deprecated technology - there is no compatibility argument against this hardening.

registry.purpose: URLAction 2402 = Allow loading of XAML files. Value 3 = Disable, 0 = Enable, 1 = Prompt. Maps to GPO 'Restricted Sites Zone > Allow loading of XAML files' = Disabled.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - takes effect on next IE / WebOC instance. Rollback: set to 0 or 1. Pair with the Internet-zone variant ('ie-zone-internet-allow-xaml') for full coverage.

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer: Allow scriptlets (Restricted Sites Zone)
findings.friendlyName: Disable scriptlets in the Restricted Sites Zone (Zone 4, URLAction 1209 = 3)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 1209 (Allow scriptlets) for Zone 4, the Restricted Sites Zone. A 'scriptlet' (.sct / .wsc) is a COM component implemented in script and exposed via the scrobj.dll handler; an HTML page can <object>-embed one and the result is arbitrary script execution wrapped in a COM facade. This pattern was the vehicle for the squiblydoo / squiblytwo LOLBin techniques (regsvr32 /n /i:http://...) and remains a popular attacker mechanism for executing JScript/VBScript inside an Office document or HTML payload while pretending to be COM activation. Setting URLAction 1209 = 3 (Disable) blocks scriptlet hosting from any Restricted-zone HTML page. The Restricted Sites Zone is the bucket Outlook puts every emailed HTML message into, so this is genuinely active surface in an HTML-mail world.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox do not host scriptlets. Restricted Sites is the riskiest-content zone; scriptlets in that zone are overwhelmingly malicious. No realistic compatibility footprint.

registry.purpose: URLAction 1209 = Allow scriptlets. Value 3 = Disable scriptlets (hardening), 0 = Enable, 1 = Prompt. Maps to GPO 'Restricted Sites Zone > Allow scriptlets' = Disabled.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - takes effect on next IE / WebOC instance. Rollback: set to 0 or 1. Combine with disabling the COM scrobj.dll handler for a defence-in-depth that catches scriptlet activation outside the browser as well.

findings.friendlyName: Disable scriptlets in the Internet Zone (Zone 3, URLAction 1209 = 3)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 1209 (Allow scriptlets) for Zone 3, the Internet Zone. Same mechanism as the Restricted-Sites variant: scriptlets (.sct / .wsc) are script-implemented COM components hosted by scrobj.dll. Allowing Internet-zone pages to embed scriptlets gives any web payload an arbitrary-script-execution primitive packaged as ordinary COM activation - exactly the technique behind regsvr32 /i:URL fileless droppers. Setting URLAction 1209 = 3 (Disable) closes the path. The Internet-zone variant has a slight Caution flavour (versus the Safe Restricted variant) only because some 1990s-era intranet sites that have somehow ended up in the Internet zone still use scriptlets for tooltip / table widgets - the right fix there is to move them to Trusted Sites via the Site-to-Zone Assignment List, not to relax this setting.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox do not host scriptlets. May break legacy intranet sites that have not been added to Trusted Sites and rely on scriptlet-based widgets - inventory IE-Mode site-list entries before enforcing.

registry.purpose: URLAction 1209 = Allow scriptlets. Value 3 = Disable, 0 = Enable, 1 = Prompt. Maps to GPO 'Internet Zone > Allow scriptlets' = Disabled.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - takes effect on next IE / WebOC instance. Rollback: set to 0 or 1. Pair with the Restricted-Sites variant ('ie-zone-restricted-allow-scriptlets') for full coverage.

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer: Allow drag and drop or copy and paste files (Restricted Sites Zone)
findings.friendlyName: Disable drag-and-drop or copy-paste of files in the Restricted Sites Zone (Zone 4, URLAction 1802 = 3)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 1802 (Allow drag and drop or copy and paste files) for Zone 4, the Restricted Sites Zone. With the default 'Enable' or 'Prompt', a Restricted-zone page can accept a file dragged from Explorer or pasted from the clipboard and read its contents via DataTransfer / clipboardData APIs. That gives an attacker-controlled HTML payload (including an emailed HTML message - which Outlook renders in this zone) the ability to silently consume any file the user drags onto a webpage banner or pastes into a comment box, and exfiltrate it. Setting URLAction 1802 = 3 (Disable) makes the IE host refuse drag-drop and clipboard file operations from Restricted-zone content entirely.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox have their own clipboard / drag-drop permission model and are unaffected. Restricted Sites is the riskiest-content zone, so denying file drag-drop / paste there has effectively no compatibility cost.

registry.purpose: URLAction 1802 = Allow drag and drop or copy and paste files. Value 3 = Disable (hardening), 0 = Enable, 1 = Prompt. Maps to GPO 'Restricted Sites Zone > Allow drag and drop or copy and paste files' = Disabled.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - takes effect on next IE / WebOC instance. Rollback: set to 0 or 1.

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer: Allow drag and drop or copy and paste files (Internet Zone)
findings.friendlyName: Disable drag-and-drop or copy-paste of files in the Internet Zone (Zone 3, URLAction 1802 = 3)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 1802 (Allow drag and drop or copy and paste files) for Zone 3, the Internet Zone. Same mechanism as the Restricted-Sites variant - allowing Internet-zone pages to accept dragged or pasted files lets a malicious HTML payload silently read any file the user drops onto a webpage 'upload here' banner. Setting URLAction 1802 = 3 (Disable) closes the path. The reason this is Caution rather than Safe in the Internet zone is that many older internal upload portals exist on Internet-zoned URLs (because nobody zoned them into Trusted Sites) and use drag-drop file upload as their primary mechanism. The right fix is to add such hosts to the Site-to-Zone Assignment List as Trusted Sites; the wrong fix is to leave 1802 wide open in the Internet zone.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox have their own permission model and are unaffected. May break legacy upload portals on Internet-zoned URLs that rely on file drag-drop / paste; inventory IE-Mode site-list entries and add them to Trusted Sites as needed before enforcing.

registry.purpose: URLAction 1802 = Allow drag and drop or copy and paste files. Value 3 = Disable, 0 = Enable, 1 = Prompt. Maps to GPO 'Internet Zone > Allow drag and drop or copy and paste files' = Disabled.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - takes effect on next IE / WebOC instance. Rollback: set to 0 or 1. Pair with the Restricted-Sites variant ('ie-zone-restricted-allow-drag-drop') for full coverage.

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer: Allow scripting of Internet Explorer WebBrowser controls (Restricted Sites Zone)
findings.friendlyName: Disable scripting of nested IE WebBrowser controls in the Restricted Sites Zone (Zone 4, URLAction 1206 = 3)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 1206 (Allow scripting of Internet Explorer WebBrowser controls) for Zone 4, the Restricted Sites Zone. URLAction 1206 governs whether script in a page can drive a nested WebBrowser ActiveX control (typically embedded by another, outer Win32 host) - reading its location, navigating it, injecting script into its document. With this enabled in the Restricted Sites Zone, a hostile HTML payload can pivot from its own renderer into a sibling WebBrowser control hosted by the same parent Win32 process and read or rewrite content there - a same-process cross-component attack that can leak authenticated session content from the parent app's other browser frames. Setting URLAction 1206 = 3 (Disable) blocks the cross-control scripting from Restricted-zone content. The Trusted-Sites Zone is normally left at 0 (Allow) for legitimate composite apps; this finding is the Restricted half. CIS / STIG / Microsoft Security Baseline all set this to Disable for Internet and Restricted zones.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox do not host nested WebBrowser controls. Restricted Sites is the riskiest-content zone and cross-control scripting from such content is overwhelmingly malicious - no realistic compatibility footprint.

registry.purpose: URLAction 1206 = Allow scripting of Internet Explorer WebBrowser controls. Value 3 = Disable (hardening for Internet/Restricted), 0 = Allow (default for Trusted Sites). Maps to GPO 'Restricted Sites Zone > Allow scripting of Internet Explorer WebBrowser controls' = Disabled.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - takes effect on next IE / WebOC instance. Rollback: set to 0. Note this URLAction is specifically about scripting nested WebBrowser controls hosted by an outer Win32 app - it does not govern ordinary intra-page script.

findings.friendlyName: Force anonymous logon for the Internet Zone (Zone 3, URLAction 1A00 = 0x00010000)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 1A00 (Logon options) for Zone 3, the Internet Zone. The 1A00 action governs how the IE / WebOC HTTP stack responds when a remote site issues an NTLM/Negotiate 401 challenge: 0 = Automatic logon with current credentials, 0x00010000 = Anonymous (never send credentials), 0x00020000 = Prompt the user once, 0x00030000 = Prompt for username/password every time. With the default of 0 in the Internet Zone, a malicious Internet-hosted page can coerce the browser into sending the user's NTLM hash (LM/NTLMv2 response) to an attacker-controlled host - the classic basis for SMB-relay-from-the-browser, hash cracking, and 'GIFShell'-style coercion chains. Setting 1A00 to 0x00010000 (Anonymous) hard-stops automatic credential transmission to any Internet-zone server. On Win11 Pro this code path is reached only through Edge IE-Mode and Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control; modern Edge has its own NTLM single-sign-on policy that is unaffected by this URLAction. The hardening value is 0x00010000 because Anonymous is the only setting that completely removes the credential-leak primitive - 'Prompt' still allows the user to supply credentials to a phishing host one click away.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox unaffected. Any IE-Mode legacy site that genuinely lives in the Internet Zone (i.e. not on the Trusted Sites or Intranet allow-list) and relies on transparent Windows-integrated authentication will start prompting the user with a basic-auth dialog or 401 errors. Correct fix is to add the legitimate site to Trusted Sites or to the IE-Mode site-list, not to relax this URLAction in the Internet Zone.

registry.name: 1A00
registry.purpose: URLAction 1A00 = Logon options. Value 0x00010000 (65536) = Anonymous logon. Maps to GPO 'Internet Zone > Logon options' = Anonymous logon.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - effective on next IE / WebOC instance. Encoding: 0=Automatic, 0x00010000 (65536)=Anonymous, 0x00020000 (131072)=Prompt, 0x00030000 (196608)=Prompt for username/password. Rollback: delete the value or set to one of the alternate encodings. The machine policy hive is the canonical GPO-managed location; the runtime mirror under HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\... is consulted only when the policy hive is absent.

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer: Logon options (Restricted Sites Zone)
findings.friendlyName: Force anonymous logon for the Restricted Sites Zone (Zone 4, URLAction 1A00 = 0x00010000)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 1A00 (Logon options) for Zone 4, the Restricted Sites Zone. Restricted Sites is the most-distrusted IE zone: it's the zone email-rendered HTML in Outlook lands in, and the zone where a hardened deployment drops sites that are explicitly known-bad. If 1A00 is left at default the browser may still attempt automatic NTLM/Negotiate against an HTML-email-embedded image URL or a 401-challenging script include - meaning an attacker can craft an HTML email that silently extracts the recipient's NTLM hash the moment Outlook renders the message. Setting 1A00 to 0x00010000 (Anonymous) ensures no credential is ever sent automatically to a Restricted-zone host, breaking the hash-leak primitive at its source. Affects only IE.exe, Edge IE-Mode, Outlook's MSHTML rendering surface and Win32 apps hosting WebBrowser/WebOC; modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox unaffected. The hardening value is 0x00010000 because for a zone defined as 'sites you do not trust', any non-Anonymous setting is a contradiction in terms.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control, and crucially Outlook's HTML email renderer. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox unaffected. By design no IE-Mode site list entry should land in Zone 4, so end-user-visible breakage is minimal; the only realistic friction is an HTML email referencing a 401-protected internal asset, which would now fail to authenticate silently - this is the desired behaviour.

registry.name: 1A00
registry.purpose: URLAction 1A00 = Logon options. Value 0x00010000 (65536) = Anonymous logon. Maps to GPO 'Restricted Sites Zone > Logon options' = Anonymous logon.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - effective on next IE / WebOC / Outlook MSHTML render. Encoding: 0=Automatic, 0x00010000 (65536)=Anonymous, 0x00020000 (131072)=Prompt, 0x00030000 (196608)=Prompt for username/password. Rollback: delete the value or set to one of the alternate encodings.

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer: Allow scripting of Internet Explorer WebBrowser controls (Internet Zone)
findings.friendlyName: Disable scripting of WebBrowser controls in the Internet Zone (Zone 3, URLAction 1206 = 3)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 1206 (Allow scripting of Internet Explorer WebBrowser controls) for Zone 3, the Internet Zone. This URLAction governs whether script in a top-level Internet-zone page is allowed to attach to and drive WebBrowser/WebOC ActiveX control instances embedded in the same page or hosted by a separate Win32 application that is rendering Internet content. With it left enabled, a malicious page can use scripting to navigate the host control to a sensitive local URL, scrape its DOM, or chain primitives across the trust boundary between the outer page and the embedded control - the same family of bugs that fed years of IE same-origin-policy bypasses. Setting URLAction 1206 to 3 (Disable) blocks cross-control scripting from Internet-zone pages. Affects only IE.exe, Edge IE-Mode and Win32 apps hosting WebBrowser/WebOC controls; modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox unaffected. The hardening value is 3 because IE URLAction encoding uses 0=Enable, 1=Prompt, 3=Disable - and any 'Prompt' for an automation-style action is unsafe because the user has no useful information to base the decision on.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox unaffected. May break legacy intranet help systems and ancient single-page apps that drove an embedded preview pane via WebBrowser-control scripting. Such apps should be migrated; if a specific app is critical, move its host into Trusted Sites where this URLAction can stay enabled.

registry.purpose: URLAction 1206 = Allow scripting of Internet Explorer WebBrowser controls. Value 3 = Disable. Maps to GPO 'Internet Zone > Allow scripting of Internet Explorer WebBrowser controls' = Disabled.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - effective on next IE / WebOC instance. URLAction encoding: 0=Enable, 1=Prompt, 3=Disable. Rollback: delete the value or set to 0.

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer: Initialize and script ActiveX controls not marked as safe (Trusted Sites Zone)
findings.friendlyName: Disable initialization of ActiveX controls not marked safe-for-scripting in the Trusted Sites Zone (Zone 2, URLAction 1201 = 3)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 1201 (Initialize and script ActiveX controls not marked as safe for scripting) for Zone 2, the Trusted Sites Zone. The 'safe-for-scripting' / 'safe-for-initialization' bits on an ActiveX control are vendor self-attestations that the control will not perform sensitive operations from arbitrary script input. Many controls that were never designed for the web (file-upload helpers, document parsers, shipping-vendor utilities) are NOT marked safe and are exactly the controls that fuel ActiveX exploitation chains. Even though Zone 2 is the curated Trusted-Sites allow-list, an attacker who poisons that list (registry tamper, Site-to-Zone misconfig, or social-engineering 'add this URL to Trusted Sites') wants this URLAction enabled so they can instantiate one of those unsafe controls. Setting URLAction 1201 to 3 (Disable) refuses to script unsafe controls even on explicitly-trusted hosts. Affects only IE.exe, Edge IE-Mode and Win32 apps hosting WebBrowser/WebOC; modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox unaffected. The hardening value is 3 because 'Prompt' (1) is meaningless to end users in the context of an opaque ActiveX init dialog, and Disable is the CIS / STIG baseline.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox unaffected. Most likely to break legacy IE-Mode line-of-business apps that were deliberately shipped with controls the vendor never bothered to mark safe-for-scripting (common in older banking / engineering / PACS / radiology / scanning workflows). If a Trusted-Sites app legitimately needs an unsafe-marked control, the right fix is to update the control or replace the app, not to relax this URLAction in the broadest-trust IE zone.

registry.purpose: URLAction 1201 = Initialize and script ActiveX controls not marked as safe for scripting. Value 3 = Disable. Maps to GPO 'Trusted Sites Zone > Initialize and script ActiveX controls not marked as safe' = Disabled.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - effective on next IE / WebOC instance. URLAction encoding: 0=Enable, 1=Prompt, 3=Disable. Rollback: delete the value or set to 0/1. Combine with the 'ie-security-zones-hklm-only' finding (Security_HKLM_only=1) so a user cannot move an exploit-bearing host into Trusted Sites via HKCU.

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer: Allow cut, copy or paste operations from the clipboard via script (Internet Zone)
findings.friendlyName: Disable scripted clipboard cut/copy/paste in the Internet Zone (Zone 3, URLAction 1407 = 3)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 1407 (Allow cut, copy or paste operations from the clipboard via script) for Zone 3, the Internet Zone. This URLAction governs whether script on an Internet-zone page can call into the legacy IE/MSHTML clipboard APIs (clipboardData.getData / setData / window.clipboardData) without an explicit user gesture. With it enabled, a malicious Internet-zone page can silently read whatever the user just copied (passwords from a password manager, credit card numbers, recovery seeds, internal URLs) or silently overwrite the clipboard with attacker-controlled content - the canonical 'pastejacking' or 'ClickFix'-style attack where an innocent-looking 'copy this code' button delivers a different payload than the one displayed. Setting URLAction 1407 to 3 (Disable) blocks scripted clipboard access entirely in the Internet Zone. Affects only IE.exe, Edge IE-Mode and Win32 apps hosting WebBrowser/WebOC; modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox use the standardised W3C Async Clipboard API which is governed by completely separate browser-level permission prompts. The hardening value is 3 because 'Prompt' (1) historically suffered from prompt-fatigue bypasses where an attacker fires the prompt repeatedly until the user clicks Allow.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox clipboard APIs are unaffected. May break legacy IE-Mode apps that implement an in-page 'Copy' button via document.execCommand('copy') or the legacy clipboardData object - an extremely common pattern in older intranet portals. If an IE-Mode app relies on it, move the app's host into Trusted Sites where this URLAction can stay enabled.

registry.purpose: URLAction 1407 = Allow cut, copy or paste operations from the clipboard via script. Value 3 = Disable. Maps to GPO 'Internet Zone > Allow cut, copy or paste operations from the clipboard via script' = Disabled.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - effective on next IE / WebOC instance. URLAction encoding: 0=Enable, 1=Prompt, 3=Disable. Rollback: delete the value or set to 0.

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer: Allow cut, copy or paste operations from the clipboard via script (Restricted Sites Zone)
findings.friendlyName: Disable scripted clipboard cut/copy/paste in the Restricted Sites Zone (Zone 4, URLAction 1407 = 3)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 1407 (Allow cut, copy or paste operations from the clipboard via script) for Zone 4, the Restricted Sites Zone - which is the zone Outlook's MSHTML renderer uses for HTML email. With this URLAction enabled, an attacker who sends an HTML email containing inline script (or script-equivalent constructs that survive Outlook's filter) can read the user's current clipboard contents (passwords, MFA codes the user just copied, recovery seeds) the moment the message is rendered, or silently replace clipboard content so a subsequent paste delivers attacker-controlled data. Setting URLAction 1407 to 3 (Disable) blocks scripted clipboard access in the Restricted Sites Zone, removing the email-rendered exfiltration primitive. Affects only IE.exe, Edge IE-Mode, Outlook's MSHTML rendering surface and Win32 apps hosting WebBrowser/WebOC; modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox use the W3C Async Clipboard API governed by separate browser-level prompts. The hardening value is 3 because for a zone explicitly defined as 'untrusted', anything other than Disable is a contradiction.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control, and crucially Outlook's HTML email renderer. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox unaffected. By design no IE-Mode site list entry should land in Zone 4, so legitimate breakage is minimal - the only realistic friction is an HTML email or a known-bad-listed site invoking document.execCommand('copy'), which is the desired behaviour to block.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - effective on next IE / WebOC / Outlook MSHTML render. URLAction encoding: 0=Enable, 1=Prompt, 3=Disable. Rollback: delete the value or set to 0.

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer: Allow only approved domains to use ActiveX controls without prompt (Restricted Sites Zone)
findings.friendlyName: Restrict ActiveX-without-prompt to admin-approved domains in the Restricted Sites Zone (Zone 4, URLAction 120B = 0x00010000)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 120B (Allow only approved domains to use ActiveX controls without prompt) for Zone 4, the Restricted Sites Zone - the zone Outlook MSHTML uses for HTML email and where known-bad sites land. With 120B left at 0 (Disable - i.e. allow any domain), any ActiveX control that has been per-site allowed elsewhere can be instantiated without a per-site prompt; combined with HTML-email rendering this means a malicious message can silently fire any pre-approved ActiveX control. Setting 120B to 0x00010000 (Enable) flips the policy so ActiveX controls require a prompt unless the hosting domain appears in the administrator-approved 'Per-Site ActiveX Controls' allow-list, dramatically narrowing the silent-instantiation surface. Affects only IE.exe, Edge IE-Mode, Outlook's MSHTML renderer and Win32 apps hosting WebBrowser/WebOC; modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox have no ActiveX surface. The hardening value is 0x00010000 because URLAction 120B uses the inverted boolean encoding common to admin-approved-list URLActions: 0 = the restriction is off, 0x00010000 = the restriction is on.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control, and Outlook's HTML email renderer. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox unaffected. Practical breakage is minimal in Zone 4 because no legitimate workflow should be rendering ActiveX from Restricted Sites; what this catches is HTML-email-borne attempts to instantiate controls and edge cases where a known-bad-listed site has previously been per-site approved.

registry.name: 120B
registry.purpose: URLAction 120B = Allow only approved domains to use ActiveX controls without prompt. Value 0x00010000 (65536) = Enable the restriction. Maps to GPO 'Restricted Sites Zone > Allow only approved domains to use ActiveX controls' = Enabled.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - effective on next IE / WebOC / Outlook MSHTML render. URLAction 120B encoding (admin-approved-list style): 0=Disabled (no restriction), 0x00010000 (65536)=Enabled (restrict to approved domains). Rollback: delete the value or set to 0. The administrator-approved list lives under HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Internet Settings\AllowedDomains.

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer: Allow only approved domains to use ActiveX controls without prompt (Internet Zone)
findings.friendlyName: Restrict ActiveX-without-prompt to admin-approved domains in the Internet Zone (Zone 3, URLAction 120B = 0x00010000)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 120B (Allow only approved domains to use ActiveX controls without prompt) for Zone 3, the Internet Zone. Without this hardening any Internet-zone page can fire any ActiveX control that has been per-site approved on the machine, even from an unrelated domain - exactly the class of bug abused by drive-by ActiveX exploitation chains where a benign third-party site is compromised and used to silently instantiate a previously-approved control. Setting 120B to 0x00010000 (Enable) requires a per-site prompt unless the hosting domain appears in the administrator-approved 'Per-Site ActiveX Controls' allow-list, breaking the cross-domain silent-instantiation primitive. Affects only IE.exe, Edge IE-Mode and Win32 apps hosting WebBrowser/WebOC; modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox have no ActiveX surface. The hardening value is 0x00010000 because URLAction 120B uses the inverted boolean encoding common to admin-approved-list URLActions: 0 = restriction off, 0x00010000 = restriction on.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox unaffected. May surface a per-site ActiveX prompt on legacy IE-Mode public-internet-facing apps that depend on a control. The right fix is to add the legitimate domain to the admin-approved AllowedDomains list, not to relax the URLAction.

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer: Don't run antimalware programs against ActiveX controls (Local Machine Zone)
findings.friendlyName: Run antimalware against ActiveX controls in the Local Machine Zone (Zone 0, URLAction 270C = 0)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 270C (Don't run antimalware programs against ActiveX controls) for Zone 0, the Local Machine Zone. The original GPO name is a confusing double-negative: a value of 0 means 'do NOT skip antimalware' - i.e. antimalware DOES scan ActiveX before instantiation - which is the secure setting. A value of 0x00010000 (or 1) means 'DO skip antimalware' - antimalware is bypassed - which is the insecure setting. With this URLAction left at the bypass value, IE / WebOC instantiates ActiveX without first asking the registered antimalware product (Defender's IOfficeAntiVirus interface) to scan the control's binary, blinding Defender / third-party AV to a major historical attack class (ActiveX-based drive-bys against local HTML files). Setting URLAction 270C to 0 forces the AM scan to run before ActiveX instantiation in the Local Machine Zone. Affects only IE.exe, Edge IE-Mode and Win32 apps hosting WebBrowser/WebOC; modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox have no ActiveX surface so the AMSI/IOfficeAntiVirus integration is a non-issue there. The hardening value is 0 because that is the only encoding that means 'always scan'.
findings.compatNote: No user-visible breakage. Causes Defender (or any registered IOfficeAntiVirus provider) to inspect each ActiveX control before it is instantiated from a Local-Machine-Zone page. The AM scan is fast and cached; only consequence is that a control your AM product flags as malicious will be blocked from loading - which is the intent.

registry.name: 270C
registry.purpose: URLAction 270C = Don't run antimalware programs against ActiveX controls. Value 0 = run antimalware (the secure setting; the GPO name is a double negative). Maps to GPO 'Local Machine Zone > Don't run antimalware programs against ActiveX controls' = Disabled (which forces the scan to run).

implementation.notes: No reboot required - effective on next IE / WebOC instance. URLAction 270C is a double-negative: 0=run antimalware (secure), 0x00010000=skip antimalware (insecure). Rollback: delete the value (defaults vary by zone) or set to 0x00010000.

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer: Don't run antimalware programs against ActiveX controls (Trusted Sites Zone)
findings.friendlyName: Run antimalware against ActiveX controls in the Trusted Sites Zone (Zone 2, URLAction 270C = 0)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 270C (Don't run antimalware programs against ActiveX controls) for Zone 2, the Trusted Sites Zone. The original GPO name is a confusing double-negative: a value of 0 means 'do NOT skip antimalware' - i.e. antimalware DOES scan ActiveX before instantiation - which is the secure setting. Trusted Sites is the IE zone administrators use to grant elevated privileges to a curated allow-list, which is precisely why an attacker who poisons that list (registry tamper, social-engineering 'add to Trusted Sites' lure) wants the AM scan skipped on instantiation. Setting URLAction 270C to 0 forces Defender (or any registered IOfficeAntiVirus provider) to inspect each ActiveX control before it is instantiated from a Trusted-Sites page, even though the zone is otherwise high-trust. Affects only IE.exe, Edge IE-Mode and Win32 apps hosting WebBrowser/WebOC; modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox have no ActiveX surface. The hardening value is 0 because that is the only encoding that means 'always scan'.
findings.compatNote: No user-visible breakage. Trusted Sites is exactly where IE-Mode line-of-business apps land, and forcing AM inspection of their ActiveX controls is defence-in-depth that does not change which controls are allowed to load - it only ensures the AM product gets to see each one. The only consequence is that a control AM flags as malicious will be blocked, which is intent.

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer: Don't run antimalware programs against ActiveX controls (Intranet Zone)
findings.friendlyName: Run antimalware against ActiveX controls in the Local Intranet Zone (Zone 1, URLAction 270C = 0)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 270C (Don't run antimalware programs against ActiveX controls) for Zone 1, the Local Intranet Zone. The original GPO name is a confusing double-negative: a value of 0 means 'do NOT skip antimalware' - i.e. antimalware DOES scan ActiveX before instantiation - which is the secure setting. The Local Intranet Zone is automatically applied to UNC paths, proxy-bypassed sites and short-name hostnames, so it is one of the easiest zones for an attacker to land in (NTLM-relayed access to an internal share, or a same-broadcast-domain machine resolved by short name). Setting URLAction 270C to 0 forces the AM scan to run before ActiveX instantiation from any Intranet-zone page, ensuring Defender / third-party AV gets a chance to block a malicious control even when the page itself is rendered from a 'trusted' internal source. Affects only IE.exe, Edge IE-Mode and Win32 apps hosting WebBrowser/WebOC; modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox have no ActiveX surface. The hardening value is 0 because that is the only encoding that means 'always scan'.
findings.compatNote: No user-visible breakage. AM inspection of intranet-zone ActiveX controls is defence-in-depth that does not change which controls are allowed to load. Only consequence is that a control AM flags as malicious will be blocked from loading, which is intent.

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer: Run .NET Framework-reliant components signed with Authenticode (Internet Zone)
findings.friendlyName: Disable execution of Authenticode-signed .NET Framework components in the Internet Zone (Zone 3, URLAction 2001 = 3)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 2001 (Run .NET Framework-reliant components signed with Authenticode) for Zone 3, the Internet Zone. This URLAction governs whether a page can instantiate browser-hosted .NET components (legacy XBAPs, IE-hosted managed controls, Authenticode-signed assemblies referenced via <object> tags) when the assembly is signed. Browser-hosted .NET surfaces have a long exploitation history (XBAP sandbox escapes, ClickOnce-via-MSHTML pivots, .NET serialization bugs reachable from a managed component) and a valid Authenticode signature does not protect against any of them - it just means the attacker either compromised a code-signing cert or socially-engineered a CA. Setting URLAction 2001 to 3 (Disable) refuses to instantiate .NET components from Internet-zone pages regardless of signature. Affects only IE.exe, Edge IE-Mode and Win32 apps hosting WebBrowser/WebOC; modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox cannot host XBAPs or managed components. The hardening value is 3 because the legitimate use case (XBAP delivery to end-users from the public internet) was deprecated by Microsoft in 2020 and removed from Edge IE-Mode entirely.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox unaffected. May break legacy XBAP / browser-hosted-WPF deployments that were delivered from the public internet - extremely rare in 2025 because Microsoft removed XBAP support from IE-Mode. If an internal XBAP is critical, move its host into Trusted Sites where this URLAction can stay enabled.

registry.purpose: URLAction 2001 = Run .NET Framework-reliant components signed with Authenticode. Value 3 = Disable. Maps to GPO 'Internet Zone > Run .NET Framework-reliant components signed with Authenticode' = Disabled.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - effective on next IE / WebOC instance. URLAction encoding: 0=Enable, 1=Prompt, 3=Disable. Rollback: delete the value or set to 0/1. The companion URLAction 2004 controls the unsigned variant and should also be set to 3.

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer: Web sites in less privileged Web content zones can navigate into this zone (Internet Zone)
findings.friendlyName: Block cross-zone navigation from less-privileged zones into the Internet Zone (Zone 3, URLAction 2101 = 3)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 2101 (Web sites in less privileged Web content zones can navigate into this zone) for Zone 3, the Internet Zone. This URLAction governs frame and window navigations that originate in a less-trusted zone (Restricted Sites, Zone 4) and target a page in the current zone. Without it, an HTML email rendered in Outlook (Zone 4) can navigate a frame, popup, or window.open() into an attacker-controlled Internet-zone page that then chains additional zone-elevation primitives - the basis of a long history of cross-zone scripting exploits in MSHTML. Setting URLAction 2101 to 3 (Disable) refuses cross-zone navigation from less-privileged sources, breaking the chain at the navigation step. Affects only IE.exe, Edge IE-Mode, Outlook's MSHTML renderer and Win32 apps hosting WebBrowser/WebOC; modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox use site-isolation and cross-origin policies that are completely separate from this URLAction. The hardening value is 3 because a 'Prompt' on a frame navigation is unusable - users cannot meaningfully evaluate whether a hidden iframe should be allowed to navigate.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control, and Outlook's HTML email renderer. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox unaffected. May break workflows where a Restricted-zone HTML email contains a click-through link that opens an Internet-zone target via frame navigation - extremely rare; mainstream 'click the link' anchor behaviour (top-level navigation in a new window) is governed by a different code path and unaffected.

registry.purpose: URLAction 2101 = Web sites in less privileged Web content zones can navigate into this zone. Value 3 = Disable. Maps to GPO 'Internet Zone > Web sites in less privileged Web content zones can navigate into this zone' = Disabled.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - effective on next IE / WebOC / Outlook MSHTML render. URLAction encoding: 0=Enable, 1=Prompt, 3=Disable. Rollback: delete the value or set to 0/1.

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer: Navigate windows and frames across different domains (Internet Zone)
findings.friendlyName: Block cross-domain navigation of windows and frames in the Internet Zone (Zone 3, URLAction 1607 = 3)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 1607 (Navigate windows and frames across different domains) for Zone 3, the Internet Zone. This URLAction governs whether script in one Internet-zone page can navigate a frame or popup window that points at a different domain. With it enabled, a malicious page can hijack the URL of a sibling iframe (a banking widget, an OAuth consent popup, a sign-in flow) and redirect it to an attacker-controlled site - the canonical 'frame navigation' attack, which Microsoft tightened across multiple IE updates and which still applies to MSHTML-hosted content. Setting URLAction 1607 to 3 (Disable) blocks cross-domain navigation of frames and windows in the Internet Zone, removing the hijack primitive. Affects only IE.exe, Edge IE-Mode and Win32 apps hosting WebBrowser/WebOC; modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox enforce far stricter same-origin and frame-ancestor rules natively, so this URLAction does not influence them. The hardening value is 3 because 'Prompt' on a script-driven frame navigation is unusable - the user cannot meaningfully evaluate the request.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox unaffected. May break legacy IE-Mode apps that legitimately drive cross-domain frame navigation - portal sites that embed federated sign-in flows, framed dashboards composed from multiple internal subdomains, or OAuth-style popups. If an IE-Mode app relies on it, move the host into Trusted Sites where this URLAction can stay enabled.

registry.purpose: URLAction 1607 = Navigate windows and frames across different domains. Value 3 = Disable. Maps to GPO 'Internet Zone > Navigate windows and frames across different domains' = Disabled.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - effective on next IE / WebOC instance. URLAction encoding: 0=Enable, 1=Prompt, 3=Disable. Rollback: delete the value or set to 0/1.

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer: Launching applications and files in an IFRAME (Internet Zone)
findings.friendlyName: Block launching applications and files from IFRAMEs in the Internet Zone (Zone 3, URLAction 1804 = 3)
findings.description: Controls URLAction 1804 (Launching applications and files in an IFRAME) for Zone 3, the Internet Zone. This URLAction governs whether content delivered inside an IFRAME can trigger application launches via Content-Type / Content-Disposition handling, mhtml: nesting, or local-protocol URIs - the exact pattern abused by IE 'mhtml' / 'res:' / 'file://' + Content-Disposition tricks to drop an executable from a hidden iframe and have the shell handler launch it. With this URLAction enabled an Internet-zone page can stage an iframe whose response triggers a file/handler launch invisibly. Setting URLAction 1804 to 3 (Disable) refuses any application or file launch initiated from inside an iframe in the Internet Zone, killing a major historical drive-by primitive. Affects only IE.exe, Edge IE-Mode and Win32 apps hosting WebBrowser/WebOC; modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox have completely separate download/handler-launch policies that are unaffected by this URLAction. The hardening value is 3 because 'Prompt' on an iframe-initiated handler launch is the exact dialog users have been trained to click through ('Open / Save / Cancel' on a download).
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe and Edge IE-Mode plus Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox unaffected. May break legacy IE-Mode apps that intentionally trigger an Office / PDF / line-of-business handler from an iframe (e.g. a framed report viewer that opens an .xls in Excel via Content-Disposition) - this is exactly the pattern the hardening targets. Migrate the workflow to a top-level navigation or move the host into Trusted Sites.

registry.purpose: URLAction 1804 = Launching applications and files in an IFRAME. Value 3 = Disable. Maps to GPO 'Internet Zone > Launching applications and files in an IFRAME' = Disabled.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - effective on next IE / WebOC instance. URLAction encoding: 0=Enable, 1=Prompt, 3=Disable. Rollback: delete the value or set to 0/1.

findings.originalName: Remove "Run this time" button for outdated ActiveX controls in Internet Explorer
findings.friendlyName: Hide the "Run this time" button when IE detects an outdated ActiveX control (HideRunThisTimeButton=1)
findings.description: When IE / Edge IE-Mode / a WebBrowser-hosting Win32 app is asked to instantiate an ActiveX control whose CLSID + version appears in Microsoft's published versionlist.xml (Java, Flash, Silverlight, Shockwave and other historically-vulnerable controls), the loader normally surfaces a notification bar offering both 'Update' and 'Run this time' - the latter lets the user one-click bypass the outdated-version block and load the known-vulnerable binary anyway, which is exactly the path drive-by exploit kits depended on for years. Enabling 'Remove the Run this time button for outdated ActiveX controls in Internet Explorer' (HideRunThisTimeButton=1) strips the bypass option from the prompt so the user can only Update or refuse, removing the 'click-once-to-own-the-box' primitive. In 2025 the standalone iexplore.exe is gone but the same MSHTML / WebOC code path is still reached through Edge IE-Mode for legacy LOB apps and any Win32 application embedding the WebBrowser control, so the hardening still has live attack-surface coverage. The compatibility risk is essentially zero in modern environments because legitimate use of an outdated, security-update-eligible ActiveX control is by definition a misconfiguration.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe, Edge IE-Mode and Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox unaffected. Only triggers when a control's CLSID + version is on Microsoft's outdated-ActiveX list (versionlist.xml) - i.e. a known-vulnerable binary - so the only 'breakage' is preventing a user from clicking past a vendor-curated 'this control is dangerous' warning, which is the desired behaviour.

registry.name: HideRunThisTimeButton
registry.purpose: Maps to GPO 'Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Internet Explorer > Security Features > Add-on Management > Remove Run this time button for outdated ActiveX controls in Internet Explorer' = Enabled.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - effective on next IE / WebOC / Edge IE-Mode instance. Rollback: delete the value or set to 0 to restore the 'Run this time' bypass button. Companion settings under HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Internet Explorer\VersionManager (e.g. DownloadVersionList=1) ensure the outdated-ActiveX list itself stays current.

findings.friendlyName: Force IE / WebOC tab content into 64-bit processes (TabProcGrowth=0, complements EPM Isolation64Bit)
findings.description: Controls how IE / Edge IE-Mode / a WebBrowser-hosting Win32 app spawns tab content processes on 64-bit Windows. Out of the box IE preferred 32-bit content processes for ActiveX compatibility, which means modern exploit-mitigation features that depend on the larger 64-bit address space (full High-Entropy ASLR, full CFG/XFG coverage, much stronger heap-spray resistance) are not in effect for the rendering process where attacker-controlled HTML / script actually runs. Setting TabProcGrowth=0 collapses tab content into a single process and, on 64-bit Windows, biases that process to 64-bit, giving attacker-controlled code the harder-to-exploit 64-bit memory layout. This finding deliberately overlaps with the Enhanced Protected Mode finding in part3 (ie-enhanced-protected-mode, which sets Isolation64Bit=1) - EPM's Isolation64Bit forces 64-bit content processes only when EPM is on, while TabProcGrowth=0 nudges the process model toward 64-bit even when EPM is not engaged, so the two settings are complementary rather than redundant. In 2025 iexplore.exe is gone but the same code paths back Edge IE-Mode and any embedded WebBrowser control, so the hardening still has live attack-surface coverage.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe, Edge IE-Mode and Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox unaffected. The 32-bit ActiveX controls historically shipped with banking smart-card middleware, document signers, scanner / capture utilities, and old PACS / radiology viewers cannot load in a 64-bit content process and will fail to instantiate. If a specific legacy LOB app needs a 32-bit-only control, exempt that app's host from EPM 64-bit and revisit TabProcGrowth - or migrate the app. See the related ie-enhanced-protected-mode finding (part3) which sets HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Internet Explorer\Main\Isolation64Bit=1 for the EPM-aware variant of the same control.

registry.name: TabProcGrowth
registry.purpose: Single tab-content process model on 64-bit Windows; biases content-process bitness to 64-bit so MSHTML/JScript runs with the full 64-bit ASLR/CFG mitigation surface. Maps to GPO 'Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Internet Explorer > Internet Control Panel > Advanced Page > Set tab process growth' = 0.

implementation.notes: No reboot required - effective on next IE / WebOC / Edge IE-Mode instance. Rollback: delete the value or set to a non-zero growth count. This finding is intentionally complementary to ie-enhanced-protected-mode in part3 which sets HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Internet Explorer\Main\Isolation64Bit=1 - apply both for full coverage (EPM-on path and EPM-off path).

findings.originalName: Internet Explorer Processes Restrict ActiveX Install
findings.friendlyName: Enable FEATURE_RESTRICT_ACTIVEXINSTALL for IE / Explorer / all processes (block on-the-fly ActiveX installs)
findings.description: Enables IE Feature Control FEATURE_RESTRICT_ACTIVEXINSTALL for iexplore.exe, explorer.exe and the wildcard '*' (all processes). When this Feature Control is on, the URLMon / WININET install path refuses to launch on-the-fly ActiveX install prompts originating from any zone other than Trusted Sites - the canonical 'a webpage wants to install this control, click Yes' dialog that powered a decade of drive-by-install attacks is suppressed entirely outside the Trusted Sites allow-list. Adding the wildcard '*' subkey extends the protection to every process that uses the IE/MSHTML/WebOC stack, not just iexplore.exe and Explorer (which historically had to be listed individually because Feature Controls are opt-in per-process). In 2025 iexplore.exe is gone but the same code path is reached through Edge IE-Mode, Outlook's MSHTML rendering surface and any Win32 application embedding the WebBrowser control - all of which inherit the FEATURE_RESTRICT_ACTIVEXINSTALL block via the wildcard entry. The hardening eliminates a major social-engineering primitive (lure user, prompt for install, get code execution) at zero cost in modern environments because legitimate ActiveX installs in 2025 are virtually nonexistent outside narrowly-scoped LOB Trusted-Sites apps.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe, Edge IE-Mode, Outlook's MSHTML renderer and Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox unaffected. Breaks legacy on-the-fly ActiveX install flows: old corporate PKI / smart-card enrolment installers, vendor plug-in 'click here to install' bootstrappers, old scanner / signer middleware delivered as ActiveX. The right fix is to push the legitimate installer through MSI / Intune / SCCM, or to add the specific install host into Trusted Sites where the Feature Control is bypassed; relaxing the Feature Control globally is not recommended.

registry.name: iexplore.exe
registry.purpose: Enables FEATURE_RESTRICT_ACTIVEXINSTALL for the iexplore.exe process; maps to the per-process slot of GPO 'Internet Explorer Processes' under 'Restrict ActiveX Install'.

registry.name: explorer.exe
registry.purpose: Enables FEATURE_RESTRICT_ACTIVEXINSTALL for the explorer.exe process so shell-hosted WebBrowser views (preview pane, folder views) also block on-the-fly ActiveX installs.
Wildcard entry - extends FEATURE_RESTRICT_ACTIVEXINSTALL to every process that uses the IE/MSHTML/WebOC stack (Outlook, embedded WebBrowser controls, Edge IE-Mode helper processes).

implementation.notes: No reboot required - effective on next process startup that loads the IE/MSHTML stack. Rollback: delete the three values or set them to 0. Trusted Sites zone bypasses this Feature Control by design - that is the intended escape hatch for legitimate intranet ActiveX installers.
