findings.friendlyName: Enable FEATURE_RESTRICT_FILEDOWNLOAD for IE / Explorer / all processes (block non-user-initiated file downloads)
findings.description: Enables IE Feature Control FEATURE_RESTRICT_FILEDOWNLOAD for iexplore.exe, explorer.exe and the wildcard '*' (all processes). When this Feature Control is on, the URLMon download path refuses to launch a file-download dialog that was not directly triggered by a user gesture (a click on a download link or button) from any zone other than Trusted Sites - so an attacker cannot use script, an iframe redirect, a window.location set, or a meta-refresh to silently push a file-save prompt at the user. Combined with FEATURE_RESTRICT_ACTIVEXINSTALL this closes the two main 'page-pushes-something-at-the-user-without-them-asking' attack primitives that drove the original drive-by-download era. Adding the wildcard '*' subkey extends the protection to every process that uses the IE/MSHTML/WebOC stack, not just iexplore.exe and Explorer. In 2025 iexplore.exe is gone but the same code path is reached through Edge IE-Mode, Outlook's MSHTML rendering surface, HTML Help (.chm) and any Win32 application embedding the WebBrowser control. The hardening's main remaining live coverage is Outlook (an HTML email cannot kick off a non-user-initiated download dialog) and embedded WebBrowser controls in third-party apps that were never re-platformed onto WebView2.
findings.compatNote: Affects only IE.exe, Edge IE-Mode, Outlook's MSHTML renderer, HTML Help / CHM and Win32 apps hosting the WebBrowser/WebOC control. Modern Edge / Chrome / Firefox unaffected. May break legacy IE-Mode LOB apps that auto-trigger a file download as part of their workflow (report generation flows where the page navigates the user straight to a generated PDF/CSV with no explicit click). The right fix is to add such hosts to Trusted Sites where the Feature Control is bypassed, or to refactor the app to require an explicit user click; relaxing the Feature Control globally is not recommended.

findings.friendlyName: Disable BCD bootdebug/debug flags (BitLocker physical-attack hardening)
findings.description: Windows Boot Configuration Data carries two debug flags that, if left on, give a physical attacker a path around TPM-only BitLocker: 'bootdebug' attaches a kernel debugger to the boot loader and 'debug' attaches one to the running kernel, both of which can be exercised pre-volume-unlock to inspect or modify the boot sequence and ultimately drop the attacker into an environment that can read protected data. Microsoft's BitLocker countermeasures and the Microsoft Security Baseline both call out disabling these as a baseline mitigation against direct-memory-access (DMA), evil-maid and cold-boot-class attacks where an attacker has physical possession of the machine. The fix is the documented 'bcdedit /set {default} bootdebug off' and 'bcdedit /set {default} debug off' pair, which writes to the live BCD store. The values stick across reboots and require physical or admin access to flip back, so they are also a useful tamper-evidence baseline to monitor.
findings.compatNote: Disables kernel and boot debugging on this machine. Legitimate impact is limited to driver developers and kernel debuggers (WinDbg over serial / 1394 / USB / network) - if you do live kernel debugging, leave these enabled and use BitLocker with a TPM+PIN or TPM+startup-key protector instead. End users see no behaviour change; reboots are unaffected. Note that the BCD store is per-bootloader-entry: '{default}' covers the typical single-OS install but if you have multi-boot / Insider / recovery entries, repeat for each identifier returned by 'bcdedit /enum'.

command.description: Disables boot loader debugging on the default boot entry. Prevents an attacker with physical access from attaching a debugger to the bootmgr/winload pre-BitLocker-unlock.
Disables kernel-mode debugging on the default boot entry. Prevents post-boot debugger attach paths that could be used to read decrypted volume contents.

implementation.notes: Effective on next boot. Rollback: 'bcdedit /set {default} bootdebug on' and 'bcdedit /set {default} debug on'. To audit other boot entries, run 'bcdedit /enum' and apply the same pair to each identifier (some recovery / Insider entries may need separate treatment). Pair with TPM+PIN BitLocker protectors and disable 1394/Thunderbolt DMA via 'Disable new DMA devices when this computer is locked' (already covered by Microsoft Security Baseline) for full physical-attack hardening.
